metadata.title: On the Collection of Isidore Mercator — the False Decretals
metadata.author: Pietro & Girolamo Ballerini
known_issues: The concluding paragraphs catalog increasingly defective or selective manuscript copies, showing progressive corruption of the textual tradition

Translation note: This is a critical scholarly text from the eighteenth century analyzing the famous medieval forgeries known as the Pseudo-Isidorian Decretals. The Ballerini brothers were among the first to systematically expose these forgeries.
The text contains extensive references to medieval manuscripts and canon law collections, which have been preserved with their technical details for scholarly accuracy.
Multiple manuscript sigla (e.g., 'Vat. 630', 'Casanatensis A 11, 14') standardized for audio but original references preserved in notes
This section is highly technical manuscript description—the Ballerinis systematically demonstrate which manuscripts contain the original Pseudo-Isidorian forgeries vs. which contain later accretions
Greek terms transliterated where they appear (though this section has few)
The phrase 'Germano-Francum' (Franco-German) is the Ballerinis' eighteenth-century terminology for the Carolingian cultural sphere; modern usage would be 'Frankish' or 'Carolingian.'
Some manuscript sigla and abbreviated references have been expanded for audio clarity while preserving scholarly precision.
Chunk 218 appears incomplete—the Latin text ends mid-sentence with 'ex lib. XII Hist. Tripartitae,' suggesting the manuscript image or transcription may be truncated.
References to 'Conciliorum' refer to earlier published volumes of conciliar acta by Hardouin, Mansi, and others
The complex argumentation about textual relationships and forgery detection has been rendered as accessibly as possible while maintaining the authors' logical structure.
Complex manuscript relationships described—audio listeners should understand the Ballerinis are comparing multiple manuscript families to establish the 'pure' text of Pseudo-Isidore

Key argument: Some forged letters traditionally attributed to Pseudo-Isidore (Damasus-Jerome correspondence, certain Leo texts) are actually later forgeries by different hands, proven by their absence from the purest manuscripts

The Ballerinis frequently cite column numbers from printed editions (e.g., 'tom. II Concil., col. 1011') which cannot be rendered audibly in a meaningful way; I have preserved them as spoken numbers for reference purposes.
Manuscript sigla (e.g., 'Vat. 630', 'Colbertinus 3368') are preserved but may be challenging for audio audiences unfamiliar with manuscript cataloging conventions.
This chunk (2 of 3) continues an argument begun in chunk 1 and will conclude in chunk 3; listeners may need context from previous sections.
Translation note: This work discusses documents attributed to early popes that were later proven to be ninth-century forgeries, though presented as ancient.
Papal numbering confusion (Felix II/III, etc.) reflects medieval uncertainty and is preserved as in the source.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The False Decretals of Isidore Mercator
chunks.latin: CAPUT VI. De collectione Isidori Mercatoris.
chunks.english: Chapter Six: On the Collection of Isidore Mercator.
chunks.notes: This chapter examines the famous medieval forgeries known as the Pseudo-Isidorian Decretals or False Decretals.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Spurious Documents and Detection of the Forgery
chunks.latin: § 1. De spuriis hujus collectiones documentis. Quam sero impostura detecta. Num Nicolaus I ejusmodi collectionem approbarit. Potiora suppositionis argumenta proponuntur.
chunks.english: Section One: On the spurious documents of this collection. How late the imposture was detected. Whether Pope Nicholas the First approved such a collection. The principal arguments for the forgery are presented.
chunks.notes: This section heading introduces the key questions about when and how these forgeries were discovered.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: 1. Collectio Isidori Mercatoris praecedentium locupletior eo praesertim titulo distinguitur, quod pluribus supposititiis documentis referta est.
chunks.english: The collection of Isidore Mercator is richer than preceding collections, and is especially distinguished by this characteristic: it is stuffed with numerous forged documents.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vetustissimorum Romanorum pontificum a S. Clemente ad S. Silvestrum usque, quorum nulla exstant decreta, plures epistolas praefert: aliisque pontificibus usque ad Gregorium Magnum aliquas item imponit, ac genuinis interserit.
chunks.english: It presents numerous letters of the most ancient Roman pontiffs from Saint Clement to Saint Sylvester—popes for whom no genuine decrees exist. It also falsely attributes certain letters to other popes up to Gregory the Great, and mixes these forgeries in with authentic documents.
chunks.notes: The forger created letters for early popes whose genuine writings had been lost to history, making detection difficult.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Mirum est tam insignem imposturam, quae non sensim, nec per partes, sed uno in libro tota simul prodiit, non statim fuisse detectam.
chunks.english: It is remarkable that such a massive imposture—which did not appear gradually or in parts, but burst forth all at once in a single book—was not immediately detected.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aliquid suspicionis suboluisse creditur Hincmaro Remensi aliisque Gallicanis episcopis qui ad Nicolaum I scripserunt.
chunks.english: Some suspicion is believed to have arisen in Hincmar, Archbishop of Rheims, and other Gallican bishops who wrote to Pope Nicholas the First.
chunks.notes: Hincmar of Rheims (circa eight hundred six to eight hundred eighty-two) was a major figure in ninth-century church politics.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Verum hi nullam falsitatis dubitationem in animum induxisse videntur, sed eo tantum nomine ipsis epistolis auctoritatem adimendam putarunt, quia, inquiebant, in codice canonum non haberentur ascriptae: nimirum quia non erant in collectione Hadriana, quam hoc tempore Ecclesiarum usu receptam Codicem canonum appellabant.
chunks.english: However, these bishops do not seem to have entertained any doubt about their falsity. Rather, they thought authority should be denied to these letters simply because, as they said, they were not recorded in the code of canons—that is, because they were not in the Hadrianic collection, which at that time was received in the usage of the churches and called the code of canons.
chunks.notes: The Collectio Hadriana was the authoritative canon law collection of the Carolingian period, sent by Pope Hadrian I to Charlemagne.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Jure autem Nicolaus ad eosdem rescribens (Nicolaus I, epist. 42 ad episc. Gall.), hanc solam rationem nihili faciendam credidit, cum non omnes Romanorum pontificum sincerissimae caeteroquin constitutiones, sed admodum paucae in ea collectione legantur; iidemque Gallicani episcopi, ubi suae intentioni haec (priorum pontificum decretalia) suffragari conspiciunt, illis indifferenter utantur.
chunks.english: But Nicholas, writing back to these same bishops in his forty-second letter to the Gallican bishops, rightly believed this argument should be dismissed as worthless, since not all of the completely genuine constitutions of the Roman pontiffs, but only very few, are found in that collection. Moreover, these same Gallican bishops, whenever they see these decretals of earlier pontiffs supporting their own purposes, make indiscriminate use of them.
chunks.notes: Pope Nicholas I (reigned eight hundred fifty-eight to eight hundred sixty-seven) defended the decretals without realizing they were forgeries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum vero illi easdem decretales epistolas, etsi in canonum codice non descriptas, ac minus acceptas traderent, non tamen spurias, sed genuinas haberent, adeo ut ipsis, cum libebat, uterentur; idem pontifex, qui illas ex sola Gallicanorum relatione cognoverat, nihil de impostura suspicatus (quandiu nihil aliunde affulgeret), repudiare non potuit; nec ejus verba quibus eas quodammodo videtur approbasse, ei praesumptioni subnixa, quam Gallicani episcopi ingesserant, pro vera ac legitima approbatione accipi queunt, quae sine ipsarum epistolarum, quibus carebat, lectione praestari non poterat.
chunks.english: But since those bishops reported that these same decretal letters, though not copied into the code of canons and less widely accepted, were nevertheless not spurious but genuine—so much so that they themselves used them when it pleased them—the same pontiff, who knew of them only from the Gallican report, suspected nothing of the imposture, as long as nothing else came to light from elsewhere. Therefore he could not reject them. Nor can his words, by which he seems in some way to have approved them, be taken as true and legitimate approval, since they were based on the presumption the Gallican bishops had thrust upon him, and such approval could not be given without actually reading the letters themselves, which he lacked.
chunks.notes: This is a crucial point: Nicholas I never actually possessed or read the forged decretals, so his apparent approval carries no weight.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Illis autem Nicolaum caruisse evidens ex eo fit, quod in suis epistolis, ubi eas allegandi multiplex fuit occasio, alia documenta sollicitius exquisita laudet, quae in canonum codice non inveniuntur; et laudet etiam quaedam apocrypha, quae olim conficta atque vulgata in antiquiores collectiones irrepserant, uti sunt synodus S. Silvestri, alia Sinvessana, gesta de purgatione Xysti III et de Polycronio: nunquam vero Pseudo-Isidorianis decretalibus usus sit.
chunks.english: That Nicholas lacked these letters is evident from the fact that in his own epistles, where there were multiple occasions to cite them, he instead carefully quotes other documents not found in the code of canons. He even cites certain apocryphal texts—such as the synod of Saint Sylvester, another from Sessa, the acts concerning the vindication of Pope Sixtus the Third, and those concerning Polychronius—texts that had long ago been forged and published and had crept into older collections. Yet he never once used the Pseudo-Isidorian decretals.
chunks.notes: This negative evidence is powerful: if Nicholas had possessed these decretals, he would certainly have cited them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Has sane Leoni IV immediato ejus praedecessori cognitas non fuisse patet ex ejus epistola ad Britannos, in qua decretalia in codice canonum contenta recensens, a Silvestro exorditur, et caetera Hadrianae collectionis ita commemorat, ut si quid negotii incurrat, quod illis dirimi nequeat, ad sanctorum Patrum Hieronymi, Augustini, etc., scripta confugiendum tradat; nec de aliis vulgatis decretalibus indicium praebeat.
chunks.english: That these decretals were also unknown to Leo the Fourth, Nicholas's immediate predecessor, is clear from his letter to the Britons. In that letter, reviewing the decretals contained in the code of canons, he begins with Sylvester and mentions the rest of the Hadrianic collection in such a way that if any business arises that cannot be resolved by them, he directs recourse to the writings of the holy fathers Jerome, Augustine, and others. He gives no indication of other published decretals.
chunks.notes: Leo IV reigned from eight hundred forty-seven to eight hundred fifty-five.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: 2. Plures quidem imposturae characteres in his decretalibus aliisque Isidorianis mercibus continentur, quae ad earum falsitatem in ipso ortu detegendam attentos et peritos homines facile inducerent.
chunks.english: Indeed, numerous marks of forgery are contained in these decretals and other Isidorian wares, which would easily have led attentive and expert men to detect their falsity at their very origin.
chunks.notes: The Ballerini brothers here introduce the evidence that should have exposed the forgeries immediately.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: At ea erat illorum temporum et sequiorum conditio, ut pauci essent qui puram remotioris antiquitatis haberent notitiam, et sanioris critices usum facere vellent aut possent.
chunks.english: But such was the condition of those times and the periods that followed, that there were few who possessed pure knowledge of more distant antiquity, and who either wished or were able to employ sound critical method.
chunks.notes: The medieval period lacked the historical-critical methods that would develop in later centuries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Primus qui earum suppositionem praesensit et indicavit, fuit cardinalis de Cusa in opere Concordantia catholica lib., III, c. 2.
chunks.english: The first person who sensed and indicated their forgery was Cardinal Nicholas of Cusa in his work the Catholic Concordance, Book Three, Chapter Two.
chunks.notes: Nicholas of Cusa (fourteen hundred one to fourteen hundred sixty-four) was the first to publicly question these documents' authenticity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Wiclefus enim, qui ante ipsum effutivit: Decretales epistolae sunt apocryphae, et seducunt a Christi fide, et clerici sunt stulti qui student eas: non solas decretales ab Isidoro confictas in suspicionem adducere, sed omnium decretalium auctoritatem evertere nitebatur.
chunks.english: For John Wycliffe, who before Cusa blurted out that 'the decretal epistles are apocryphal and lead astray from the faith of Christ, and clerics who study them are fools'—he was not trying merely to cast suspicion on the decretals forged by Isidore, but rather was striving to overthrow the authority of all decretals.
chunks.notes: Wycliffe (circa thirteen hundred thirty to thirteen hundred eighty-four) rejected papal authority generally, not just the forgeries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Post Cusanum vero sensim alii plures excitati, adeo multa et firma ad suppositionem probandam attulerunt in medium, ut nemo jam de imposturis ejusmodi jure possit ambigere.
chunks.english: But after Cusa, gradually many others were stirred up and brought forward so many strong proofs of the forgery that no one can now reasonably doubt such impostures.
chunks.notes: By the eighteenth century when the Ballerini brothers wrote, the forgeries were universally recognized by scholars.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: 3. Nonnulla imposturae argumenta satis sit hic delibare.
chunks.english: It will suffice here to sketch out some of the arguments for the forgery.
chunks.notes: The Ballerini brothers now present their evidence systematically.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Non leve est illud quod ex ipsa praefatione proditur. Haec in titulo inscribitur sancto Isidoro episcopo, quem quidem episcopum etiam declarat illa ipsa formula praefationi inserta, qua episcopos fratres nostros appellat.
chunks.english: Not trivial is the evidence that emerges from the preface itself. The title is inscribed to Saint Isidore the bishop, and the formula inserted in the preface itself also declares him a bishop, since it calls bishops 'our brothers.'
chunks.notes: Only a bishop would call other bishops 'our brothers' in the formal language of the period.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hispanum vero praeferunt illa ipsa praefationis verba: In principio vero voluminis hujus qualiter concilium APUD NOS celebretur, posuimus: subditur autem post praefationem ordo celebrandi concilii sumptus ex synodo Toletana IV, c. 4.
chunks.english: But these very words of the preface show him to be Spanish: 'At the beginning of this volume we have placed how a council is celebrated among us.' After the preface is added an order for celebrating a council taken from the Fourth Synod of Toledo, Chapter Four.
chunks.notes: The Fourth Council of Toledo met in six hundred thirty-three, presided over by Isidore of Seville.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quis autem alius Isidorus Hispanus, et episcopus, et sanctus, nisi celebris ille S. Isidorus episcopus Hispalensis cui quidem eamdem collectionem vix editam, id est nono saeculo, ex eadem utique praefatione vulgo fuisse ascriptam satis cognoscitur ex Hincmari Remensis epist. 7, c. 12?
chunks.english: Who else could this Spanish Isidore, both bishop and saint, be except the famous Saint Isidore, Bishop of Seville? That this same collection, scarcely published—that is, in the ninth century—was commonly attributed to him on the basis of this very preface is sufficiently known from Hincmar of Rheims's seventh letter, Chapter Twelve.
chunks.notes: Isidore of Seville (circa five hundred sixty to six hundred thirty-six) was one of the most learned men of his age, making him an ideal name to attach to a forgery.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quae autem major impostura quam S. Isidoro Hispalensi episcopo collectionem tribuere octo Toletana concilia continentem a V ad XIII, et unum Bracarense, quae post ipsius mortem celebrata fuerunt; et eodem anachronismo in ipsa praefatione mentionem facere concilii VI oecumenici, quod S. Isidoro vivente nondum habitum fuerat?
chunks.english: But what greater imposture could there be than to attribute to Saint Isidore, Bishop of Seville, a collection containing eight Toledan councils from the Fifth to the Thirteenth, and one council of Braga, all of which were celebrated after his death? And by the same anachronism, to make mention in the preface itself of the Sixth Ecumenical Council, which had not yet been held while Saint Isidore was alive?
chunks.notes: This is devastating evidence: the collection contains councils that happened decades after Isidore's death in six hundred thirty-six.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid quod in epistolis quae affiguntur priscis trium priorum saeculorum pontificibus, plura sacrae Scripturae testimonia leguntur ex versione S. Hieronymi, quae posterius edita fuit?
chunks.english: What about the fact that in the letters attached to ancient pontiffs of the first three centuries, numerous testimonies of Sacred Scripture are read from the version of Saint Jerome, which was published later?
chunks.notes: Jerome's Vulgate translation was completed circa four hundred, making it impossible for first-century popes to have quoted it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid quod pleraeque consulares notae iisdem epistolis appensae vel consules referunt, qui in fastis consularibus nunquam fuerunt, vel si inveniuntur in fastis, cum pontificis epistolae auctoris aetate nequaquam concordant?
chunks.english: What about the fact that most of the consular dates appended to these same letters either cite consuls who never appear in the consular lists, or if they are found in the lists, they do not at all match the age of the pontiff who supposedly authored the letter?
chunks.notes: Ancient Roman letters were dated by the names of the consuls serving that year; the forger got these chronologies badly wrong.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Adde magistratuum conficta nomina, archiflaminum, primorum flaminum, etc., et voces primatuum, archiepiscoporum, apocrisiaricorum, quae ab aetate ac usu veterum illorum pontificum omnino aliena quisque vel leviter in antiqua historia et disciplina ecclesiastica versatus facile intelliget.
chunks.english: Add the invented names of magistrates—archiflamens, chief flamens, and so forth—and the terms primates, archbishops, and apocrisiaries, which anyone even slightly versed in ancient history and ecclesiastical discipline will easily understand to be completely foreign to the age and usage of those ancient pontiffs.
chunks.notes: These are medieval ecclesiastical titles that did not exist in the early church period the letters supposedly come from.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Adde quod nullum priscorum morum indicium, plurima autem posterioris disciplinae in iisdem litteris continentur.
chunks.english: Add that the letters contain no indication of ancient customs, but very many features of later discipline.
chunks.notes: The content reflects ninth-century church practice, not early Christian practice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Alia non pauca argumenta alii uberius notarunt, quae referre nec vacat, nec opus est.
chunks.english: Others have noted quite a few other arguments more fully, which there is neither time nor need to recount here.
chunks.notes: The Ballerini brothers are summarizing; fuller treatments existed in other works they reference.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: 4. Duo tantum omittenda non credimus, quae licet seorsim aliquam responsionem possint recipere, simul tamen juncta evidentem imposturae demonstrationem praeseferunt.
chunks.english: We believe two points alone must not be omitted, which, although separately they might admit some response, nevertheless when joined together present an evident demonstration of the imposture.
chunks.notes: This introduces the most powerful combined argument.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Primo aliae ex his epistolis datae traduntur ad omnes episcopos, aliae ad orthodoxos omnes, ad omnes Ecclesias, ad episcopos Italiae, Galliae, Hispaniae, Africae, Germaniae, etc. Plura ergo earum exemplaria per orbem disseminata esse debuerunt.
chunks.english: First, some of these letters are said to have been addressed to all bishops, others to all the orthodox, to all churches, to the bishops of Italy, Gaul, Spain, Africa, Germany, and so forth. Therefore many copies of them ought to have been disseminated throughout the world.
chunks.notes: If these were genuine papal letters to entire regions, copies would have survived in many places.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui autem fieri potuit ut e tot epistolis toto orbe divulgatis ne una quidem ante nonum saeculum a quopiam inventa et laudata fuerit?
chunks.english: But how could it have happened that out of so many letters published throughout the world, not even one was found and cited by anyone before the ninth century?
chunks.notes: The total silence of all earlier sources is inexplicable if the letters were genuine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Secundo eaedem epistolae posteriorum quarti, quinti, sexti, septimi et octavi etiam saeculi auctorum non sententias solum, sed voces, et integras periodos satis prolixas et multas exscriptas exhibent, uti accurate post Blondellum P. Philippus Labbeus in margine earumdem epistolarum notavit.
chunks.english: Second, these same letters display not only the ideas but also the words and entire quite lengthy and numerous periods copied verbatim from authors of the later fourth, fifth, sixth, seventh, and even eighth centuries, as Father Philippe Labbé carefully noted in the margins of these same letters after David Blondel.
chunks.notes: The forger copied whole paragraphs from much later writers, a clear sign of forgery.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Primo argumento responderi potest eas epistolas usque ad nonum saeculum latuisse, et idcirco a nemine fuisse laudatas. Secundo autem non nemo respondit eos posteriores auctores qui indicatas sententias iisdem verbis praeferunt, eas ex ipsis epistolis ad verbum exscripsisse.
chunks.english: To the first argument it can be replied that these letters lay hidden until the ninth century, and therefore were cited by no one. To the second, some have responded that those later authors who present the indicated ideas in the same words copied them verbatim from the letters themselves.
chunks.notes: The Ballerini brothers now address potential objections to their argument.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Neutra vero responsio probabilis est: immo altera cum altera pugnat.
chunks.english: But neither response is plausible. Indeed, one fights against the other.
chunks.notes: The two defenses are mutually contradictory.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si enim epistolae statim post earum ortum usque ad nonum saeculum ita delituissent, ut nemo idcirco eas allegare potuerit; posterioribus ergo scriptoribus non patuerunt, ut ex iisdem possent periodos exscribere.
chunks.english: For if the letters had lain so hidden from immediately after their origin until the ninth century that no one could therefore cite them, then they were not available to later writers so that they could copy passages from them.
chunks.notes: If the letters were hidden, later writers couldn't have quoted them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod si ex ipsis periodi integrae a pluribus multo et temporis et locorum intervallo dissitis fuerunt excerptae, in tenebris ergo diutius fuisse sepultae dici nequeunt.
chunks.english: But if entire passages from them were excerpted by many writers separated by great intervals of time and place, then they cannot be said to have been buried in darkness for a long time.
chunks.notes: If the letters were widely known, they would have been cited.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Alterutrum malis, semper impostura convincitur, ac ex ipso responsionum conflictu magis multo elucescit.
chunks.english: Whichever option you choose, the imposture is always convicted, and from the very conflict of the responses it shines forth much more clearly.
chunks.notes: This is the logical trap: both explanations fail, proving forgery.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si enim plures antiqui scriptores ex ipsis epistolis sumpsissent sententias, verba atque periodos; notae igitur fuissent, et frequenti usu tritae, rebusque pluribus utiles, ita ut laudari possent a multis, et ab iis praesertim qui ex ipsis profecissent: inter quos sane summi pontifices, et synodi, et Patres, qui eadem verba et easdem periodos praeferunt, ita has vetustissimorum pontificum epistolas allegassent, uti alias aliorum, quae genuinae sunt, laudasse inveniuntur.
chunks.english: For if many ancient writers had taken ideas, words, and passages from the letters themselves, then the letters would have been well known, worn by frequent use, and useful for many purposes, so that they could have been cited by many—especially by those who had benefited from them. Among these, certainly the supreme pontiffs, synods, and fathers who present the same words and the same passages would have cited these letters of the most ancient pontiffs just as they are found to have cited other genuine letters of others.
chunks.notes: Genuine papal letters were regularly cited; these never were until the ninth century.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si vero inter tam multos nono saeculo anteriores viros et sanctissimos, et doctrina celebres, qui easdem sententias iisdem verbis in suis operibus afferunt, ne unus quidem inventus est qui illarum mentionem fecerit, quia ex ipsis reipsa nihil sumpserunt; sententiae ergo et periodi pares, quae in epistolis quoque inveniuntur, ex iisdem procul dubio scriptoribus derivatae fuerunt ab epistolarum artifice; quod spuriam earum originem clare demonstrat.
chunks.english: But if among so many most holy and doctrinally celebrated men before the ninth century who present the same ideas in the same words in their own works, not even one has been found who mentioned these letters—because they actually took nothing from them—then the matching ideas and passages that are also found in the letters were undoubtedly derived by the forger of the letters from these same writers, which clearly demonstrates their spurious origin.
chunks.notes: The forger copied from genuine writers, not vice versa—the only explanation that fits the evidence.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc etiam a nullo alio auctore ante nonum saeculum fuerunt laudatae, non quia latuerunt, aut earum allegandarum materies et occasio non esset; sed quia nondum natus erat qui easdem confingeret: alias sicut illas post hoc tempus quo suppositae et vulgatae fuerunt, passim laudatas, et in collectiones etiam non pauca ex iisdem traducta reperimus; ita idipsum antiquis etiam temporibus factum deprehenderemus, si a priscis illis pontificibus quibus affictae sunt, vere scriptae et editae fuissent.
chunks.english: Hence they were also cited by no other author before the ninth century, not because they lay hidden or because there was no subject matter or occasion for citing them, but because the person who would forge them had not yet been born. Otherwise, just as we find them widely cited after the time when they were forged and published, and quite a few passages from them incorporated into collections, we would discover the same thing happening in ancient times as well, if they had truly been written and published by those ancient pontiffs to whom they are attributed.
chunks.notes: This clinches the argument: the forgeries were created in the ninth century, which explains the pattern of citations perfectly.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Genuine Documents in the Collection and Their Sources
chunks.latin: § 2. Alia documenta sincera eidem collectioni inserta, quibus e fontibus hausta fuerint. Quanti intersit hos fontes detegere ad ea discernenda quae Pseudo-Isidorus confinxit. Quaedam sincera documenta ab eodem interpolata. Duae ultimae synodi Symmacho attributae, et capitula Hadriano l asserta, quae genuina a multis creduntur, ab eodem Pseudo-Isidoro supposita demonstrantur.
chunks.english: Section Two: Other genuine documents inserted in the same collection, from what sources they were drawn. How important it is to discover these sources for distinguishing what Pseudo-Isidore forged. Certain genuine documents interpolated by him. The last two synods attributed to Pope Symmachus, and the chapters asserted to Pope Hadrian the First, which many believe genuine, are demonstrated to have been forged by this same Pseudo-Isidore.
chunks.notes: This section examines the mixture of genuine and forged material in the collection.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: 5. Neque vero ex solis documentis spuriis abs se consarcinatis Pseudo-Isidorus suam collectionem condidit. Plura scilicet sincerissima et aliunde nota monumenta falsis intermiscuit.
chunks.english: Pseudo-Isidore did not construct his collection from spurious documents patched together by himself alone. Rather, he mixed many completely genuine and otherwise known monuments in with the forgeries.
chunks.notes: This mixing of genuine and forged material made detection much more difficult.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Fontes autem e quibus haec sincera documenta derivavit detegendi sunt; id enim non modicum juverit ad ea ipsa secernenda quae ille e suo cerebro commentus est.
chunks.english: The sources from which he derived these genuine documents must be uncovered, for this will help not a little in distinguishing those very things which he invented from his own brain.
chunks.notes: Source criticism is essential for separating genuine from forged material.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praecipuus fons est collectio Hispanica, quam totam suae collectioni inseruit.
chunks.english: The principal source is the Hispanic collection, which he inserted in its entirety into his own collection.
chunks.notes: The Collectio Hispanica was a genuine Spanish canon law collection.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque vulgata esset opinio S. Isidorum Hispalensem fuisse auctorem collectionis Hispanicae, hinc Isidori nomen, quod omnibus venerationi erat, libenter arripuit et praefationi inscripsit.
chunks.english: And since the common opinion was that Saint Isidore of Seville had been the author of the Hispanic collection, he gladly seized upon the name of Isidore—which was venerated by all—and inscribed it to his preface.
chunks.notes: The forger exploited Isidore's reputation to lend credibility to the forgeries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: cap. 2, n. 8, decernens ut per singulos annos sanctum oleum in coena Domini juxta traditionem apostolicam ac statuta decretalium, in quo de eadem re praecipitur, ab episcopis conficiatur, secundam Fabiani pseudoepistolam indicare creditur. Hinc ea editio post annum [Col. 0249C] 829 et ante annum 836 statuenda videtur.
chunks.english: Chapter two, number eight, decrees that every year the sacred oil should be consecrated by bishops on Maundy Thursday according to apostolic tradition and the statutes of the decretals, in which the same matter is commanded. This is believed to indicate the second pseudo-epistle of Fabian. Therefore, this edition seems to have been established after the year eight hundred twenty-nine but before eight hundred thirty-six.
chunks.notes: Technical discussion of dating of Pseudo-Isidorian texts based on references to Fabian's letters.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Mirum vero est Rabanum, qui paulo post annum 841 Otgario, seu Autcario Moguntino episcopo Poenitentiale nuncupavit, nihil ex apocryphis Isidorianis produxisse. Num id fecerit quia ipsis nihil fidebat, an vero quia nondum prodiissent, ita ut Aquisgranensis concilii decretum ad Fabiani pseudoepistolam perperam referatur, alii judicent.
chunks.english: It is indeed remarkable that Rabanus, who shortly after eight forty-one dedicated his Penitential to Otgar (or Autcar), bishop of Mainz, produced nothing from the Isidorian forgeries. Whether he did this because he placed no trust in them, or rather because they had not yet appeared (so that the decree of the Council of Aachen would be incorrectly attributed to the pseudo-epistle of Fabian), let others judge.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod si hoc secundum probabilius judicetur, et multo magis si cum Blondello credatur impostor quasdam formulas et phrases excerpsisse ex epistola Jonae Aurelianensis ad Carolum Calvum, qui regnum obtinuit anno 839, Isidoriana collectio vulgata dicenda esset circa tempus quo Benedictus levita libros Capitularium edidit, [CCXXIV] nimirum circa annum 845, unde prima apocryphorum expressa mentio legitur in epistola Caroli Calvi ad episcopos in conventu Carisiaco scripta anno 857.
chunks.english: But if this second option is judged more probable, and much more so if one believes with Blondel that the impostor excerpted certain formulas and phrases from the letter of Jonas of Orléans to Charles the Bald, who obtained the kingdom in eight thirty-nine, then the Isidorian collection would have to be dated as published around the time when Benedict the Levite published his books of Capitularies—namely, around eight forty-five. The first explicit mention of the forgeries is found in the letter of Charles the Bald to the bishops at the assembly of Quierzy, written in eight fifty-seven.
chunks.notes: Dating the Pseudo-Isidorian collection based on textual dependencies and earliest citations.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Neque vero moveat collectio Remedio Curiensi attributa et a Goldasto edita cum hac epigraphe: Alamannicae Ecclesiae veteris canones ex pontificum epistolis [Col. 0249D] excerpti a Remedio Curiensi episcopo jussu Karoli Magni regis Francorum et Alamannorum (T. II Rer. Alamann. part. II, p. 134, edit. 1730).
chunks.english: Nor should one be troubled by the collection attributed to Remedius of Chur and published by Goldast with this inscription: 'Canons of the ancient Alemannic Church, excerpted from the epistles of the pontiffs by Remedius, bishop of Chur, at the command of Charles the Great, king of the Franks and Alemanni' (Volume Two of the Alemannic Affairs, part two, page one hundred thirty-four, edition of seventeen thirty).
chunks.notes: Discussing another medieval collection sometimes confused with Pseudo-Isidore.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hic rex cum obierit anno 814, Goldastus ipsam collectionem conditam putat circa annum 813. Amplior erat haec collectio, eique additamenta accesserunt Notingi Constantiensis episcopi, qui vita functus est anno 934.
chunks.english: Since this king died in eight fourteen, Goldast thinks the collection itself was composed around eight thirteen. This collection was more extensive, and additions were made to it by Notting, bishop of Constance, who died in nine thirty-four.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: At hae Notingi additiones cum non exigua parte canonum a Remedio collectorum ex suo codice excisa atque sublata idem Goldastus tradit. Capita ab eodem edita quae supersunt, sunt tantum quadraginta novem; eaque ex ordine exhibent excerpta ex solis pseudoepistolis priscorum pontificum a S. Clemente ad S. Urbanum usque.
chunks.english: But these additions of Notting, along with a not inconsiderable part of the canons collected by Remedius, were cut out and removed from his manuscript, as the same Goldast reports. The chapters published by him that survive are only forty-nine in number, and these display in order excerpts from only the pseudo-epistles of the ancient pontiffs from Saint Clement to Saint Urban.
chunks.notes: Showing that this collection drew from Pseudo-Isidore, not the other way around.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec vero ipsa excerpta, quae auctor ex Pseudo-Isidori collectione post Carolum Magnum, ut vidimus, compacta derivavit, palam faciunt errorem esse in titulo vel in explicatione tituli Karoli Magni, et Remedium, qui horum excerptorum [Col. 0250A] auctor traditur, ad posteriorem aetatem referri oportere.
chunks.english: But these very excerpts, which the author derived from the Pseudo-Isidorian collection compiled after Charlemagne (as we have seen), clearly demonstrate that there is an error in the title or in the interpretation of the title concerning Charles the Great, and that Remedius, who is reported as the author of these excerpts, must be assigned to a later age.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Forte pro Carolo Magno intelligendus est Carolus Crassus, qui ita Carolus Magnus fuerit nuncupatus, uti Ludovicus Magnus dictus invenitur imperator Ludovicus II. Carolus nimirum Crassus vulgata jam Pseudo-Isidori collectione primo Alemannorum rex fuit, et postea imperium obtinuit.
chunks.english: Perhaps instead of Charles the Great, we should understand Charles the Fat, who may have been called Charles the Great just as Emperor Louis the Second is found to be called Louis the Great. Charles the Fat was first king of the Alemanni (after the Pseudo-Isidorian collection had already been published), and later obtained the empire.
chunks.notes: Proposing a chronological solution to the apparent anachronism.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Evidence for the Frankish Origin of Pseudo-Isidore
chunks.latin: 14. Pseudo-Isidorum vero non fuisse Hispanum, sed Germano-Francum, multa convincunt.
chunks.english: Section fourteen. Many proofs demonstrate that Pseudo-Isidore was not Spanish, but Franco-German.
chunks.notes: Major transitional argument about the geographic origin of the forgery.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ex Moguntia quidem collectionem prodiisse, et per Gallias disseminata fuisse exemplaria Hincmaro teste accepimus: qui licet in eo falsus sit, quod praesumens auctorem S. Isidorum Hispalensem, eam ex Hispania allatam tradidit; in eo tamen quod e Moguntia eadem collectio vulgata fuerit, collato Benedicti levitae testimonio maxime confirmatur.
chunks.english: We have learned from Hincmar's testimony that the collection came forth from Mainz and that copies were disseminated throughout Gaul. Although he was mistaken in presuming the author to be Saint Isidore of Seville and thus reporting that it had been brought from Spain, nonetheless his claim that the same collection was published from Mainz is strongly confirmed by comparing it with the testimony of Benedict the Levite.
chunks.notes: Hincmar is a key contemporary witness, though mistaken about Spanish origin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hac de causa, qui apocrypha illa nono saeculo primum laudarunt, Germano-Galli sunt, nulli autem Hispani, aut aliarum regionum scriptores.
chunks.english: For this reason, those who first cited these forgeries in the ninth century are Franco-Germans; none are Spaniards or writers from other regions.
chunks.notes: Pattern of early citations points to Franco-German origin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hispanos quidem hanc collectionem diutius ignorasse vel non suscepisse colligere [Col. 0250B] licet ex epist. 121 Innocentii III, lib. II. Hinc enim discimus eos in quadam causa Alexandro III obtulisse suum codicem canonum, in quo cum aliis conciliis describebatur Emeritense.
chunks.english: Indeed, we may gather from the one hundred twenty-first epistle of Innocent the Third, book two, that the Spaniards either remained ignorant of this collection for a long time or did not accept it. From this we learn that in a certain case they presented to Alexander the Third their own codex of canons, in which the Council of Emerita was described along with other councils.
chunks.notes: Spanish Church preferred the authentic Hispanic collection into the twelfth century.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hoc autem concilium cum aliis non in Isidoriana collectione, sed in auctiori Hispanica describitur: ac proinde corpus canonum per id quoque tempus apud Hispanos receptum erat collectio Hispanica.
chunks.english: But this council, along with others, is described not in the Isidorian collection but in the fuller Hispanic collection. Therefore, the body of canons received among the Spaniards even at that time was the Hispanic collection.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vetustiores codices Isidoriani sunt Germano-Franci, adeo ut ii quoque qui in alias regiones traducti nonum saeculum attingunt, e Galliis provenerint, ut de duobus Vaticanis nono saeculo scriptis paragrapho sequenti patebit.
chunks.english: The oldest Isidorian manuscripts are Franco-German, so much so that even those which were carried to other regions and reach back to the ninth century came from Gaul, as will be clear in the next paragraph regarding two Vatican manuscripts written in the ninth century.
chunks.notes: Codicological evidence supports Gallic origin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Adde quod in pseudoepistolis plures Germano-Francorum idiotismi inveniuntur, eademque styli barbaries quae in aliis ejusdem aetatis scriptoribus Gallicanis et Germanis deprehenditur; idiotismi autem Hispanorum proprii, qui in Etherio et Eulogio notari queunt, nulli.
chunks.english: Add to this that in the pseudo-epistles many Franco-German idioms are found, along with the same stylistic barbarisms that are detected in other Gallic and German writers of the same age. But the distinctive Spanish idioms that can be noted in Ethereus and Eulogius are completely absent.
chunks.notes: Linguistic analysis confirms non-Spanish authorship.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Evidentius est illud, quia impostor non pauca transcripsit ex epistolis S. Bonifacii episcopi Moguntini, et [Col. 0250C] ex litteris Cangith abbatissae ad eumdem Bonifacium, quae non in Hispania, sed Moguntiae et in Germania, ubi illi versati sunt, inveniri potuerunt.
chunks.english: More evident still is this fact: the impostor transcribed not a few passages from the letters of Saint Boniface, bishop of Mainz, and from the letters of Abbess Bugga to the same Boniface. These could only have been found not in Spain but in Mainz and Germany, where they lived and worked.
chunks.notes: Source dependencies prove German location of forger.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Accedit tandem argumentum omnium, ut nobis quidem videtur, gravissimum. Dum impostor Hispanicam collectionem suae inseruit, non usus est codicibus Hispanae originis, sed illis originis Gallicanae.
chunks.english: Finally, there is added what seems to us the weightiest argument of all. When the impostor inserted the Hispanic collection into his own, he did not use manuscripts of Spanish origin, but those of Gallic origin.
chunks.notes: Most decisive proof: the forger used Gallic manuscripts of Spanish councils.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Id nobis innotuit in conferendis epistolis S. Leonis. Duos enim codices collectionis Hispanicae adhibuimus, Vindebonensem 41, originis Hispanae, ac Vaticanum 1341, Gallicanae originis.
chunks.english: This became known to us when collating the epistles of Saint Leo. For we consulted two manuscripts of the Hispanic collection: Vienna manuscript forty-one, of Spanish origin, and Vatican manuscript thirteen forty-one, of Gallic origin.
chunks.notes: Detailed textual comparison revealing manuscript lineage.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Insignem horum codicum varietatem in lectionibus jam notavimus cap. 4, n. 13, ita ut in Vindobonensi corruptae quidem sint, sed ad veram lectionem ipsi errores accedant; in Vaticano autem manifestae sint emendationes arbitrariae, quae a germano textu recedunt.
chunks.english: We have already noted the striking variety in readings between these manuscripts in chapter four, number thirteen. In the Vienna manuscript the readings are indeed corrupt, but the very errors approach the true reading; whereas in the Vatican manuscript there are manifest arbitrary emendations that depart from the authentic text.
chunks.notes: Spanish MS preserves older corrupt readings; Gallic MS shows editorial alterations.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Huic autem Vaticano codici, qui ex Gallia in bibliothecam Vaticanam pervenit, similes plane in lectionibus seu emendationibus agnovimus tres alios codices Gallicanos laudatos a P. Coustantio, ex quibus ejusmodi codices in Galliis inventi ac disseminati, [Col. 0250D] a studioso aliquo Gallicano antiquario Hispanicum corruptum codicem olim exscribente et emendante provenire nemo dubitare potest. Vide quae in hanc rem annotavimus loco laudato.
chunks.english: To this Vatican manuscript, which came from Gaul into the Vatican library, we recognized three other Gallic manuscripts cited by Father Coustant as completely similar in readings and emendations. From these it is clear that such manuscripts found and disseminated in Gaul originated from some studious Gallic antiquarian who once copied and emended a corrupt Spanish manuscript. See what we have noted on this matter in the place cited.
chunks.notes: Tracing the textual genealogy of Gallic copies of Spanish councils.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Porro easdem [Col. 0250D] Leonis epistolas contulimus cum editione Merlini quae e ms. Isidoriano prodiit, nec non cum aliis codicibus Isidorianis, ac praesertim cum Vat. 630, qui inter Isidorianos omnium vetustissimus et Pseudo-Isidoro suppar puriorem ejus collectionis continet.
chunks.english: Moreover, we have collated these same epistles of Leo with the edition of Merlin that came forth from an Isidorian manuscript, as well as with other Isidorian manuscripts, and especially with Vatican manuscript six hundred thirty, which among Isidorian manuscripts is the most ancient of all and, being contemporary with Pseudo-Isidore, contains a purer version of his collection.
chunks.notes: Establishing VAT 630 as the earliest and most authentic witness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Horum autem lectiones cum plurimum absunt a lectionibus Vindobonensis exempli originis Hispanae; tum easdem omnino lectiones et emendationes repraesentant quae sunt in codice Vat. 1341 originis Gallicanae: idque adeo evidenter patuit, ut conferentibus codicem Isidorianum Vat. 630 cum Hispanico Vat. 1341 in eisdem Leoninis epistolis, alter ex altero ad verbum exscriptus visus sit.
chunks.english: But the readings of these manuscripts, while they differ greatly from the readings of the Vienna manuscript of Spanish origin, represent exactly the same readings and emendations that are found in Vatican manuscript thirteen forty-one of Gallic origin. And this was so evident that when we compared the Isidorian manuscript Vatican six thirty with the Hispanic manuscript Vatican thirteen forty-one in the same Leonine epistles, one appeared to have been copied word for word from the other.
chunks.notes: Smoking gun evidence: Pseudo-Isidore's text matches Gallic, not Spanish, manuscripts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nihil ergo dubitandum est quin Pseudo-Isidorus in inserendis [Col. 0251A] suae collectioni documentis collectionis Hispanicae codicem adhibuerit non Hispanae originis, sed Gallicanae.
chunks.english: Therefore, there should be no doubt that Pseudo-Isidore, when inserting documents from the Hispanic collection into his own, employed a manuscript not of Spanish origin but of Gallic origin.
chunks.notes: Definitive conclusion of the textual argument.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid ita? nisi quia in Galliis, ubi erant hujusmodi codices, non autem in Hispania suam collectionem digessit. Quis ergo post tot ac tam manifesta indicia de auctore Gallicano, seu Germano-Franco adhuc ambigendum putet?
chunks.english: Why so? Because he compiled his collection in Gaul, where such manuscripts existed, and not in Spain. Who then, after so many and such manifest proofs, could still doubt that the author was Gallic, or Franco-German?
chunks.notes: Rhetorical conclusion after cumulative proof.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Description of Isidorian Manuscripts and Document Catalog
chunks.latin: § 5. De codicibus Isidorianis. Ordo et catalogus documentorum hujus collectionis sumptus ex antiquissimo omnium ms. Vat. 630. Spuria, sincera et interpolata, accurate discernuntur.
chunks.english: Section five. On the Isidorian manuscripts. The order and catalog of documents from this collection, taken from the most ancient of all manuscripts, Vatican six thirty. Spurious, genuine, and interpolated texts are carefully distinguished.
chunks.notes: Beginning detailed codicological analysis.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: 15. Cum mss. codices Isidorianae collectionis in bibliothecis frequentiores sunt; tum vero plerique ita inter se discrepant, ut quid contineret primitiva collectio plures dubitent.
chunks.english: Section fifteen. Since manuscript codices of the Isidorian collection are quite common in libraries, yet most of them differ so much among themselves, many have doubted what the original collection actually contained.
chunks.notes: Introducing the manuscript tradition's complexity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Multi enim codices solas decretales epistolas praeferunt, carent vero canonibus conciliorum, quos tamen Pseudo-Isidorum suae collectioni inseruisse ex ejus praefatione liquet.
chunks.english: For many manuscripts present only the decretal epistles and lack the canons of the councils, which nevertheless are clearly stated in his preface to have been inserted by Pseudo-Isidore into his collection.
chunks.notes: Some MSS are incomplete or represent later abbreviations.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In his alii plures, alii pauciores epistolas continent, alii [Col. 0251B] etiam ordine ipsarum nonnunquam discrepant. Qui autem canones cum epistolis exhibent, non semper conveniunt.
chunks.english: Among these, some contain more epistles, others fewer, and some even occasionally differ in their order. Those that present the canons along with the epistles do not always agree.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quidam enim auctiores sunt in epistolis, quas vel Isidorianis inserunt, vel in fine adjiciunt. Nobis tredecim exemplaria invenire licuit, e quibus quinque conciliorum canonibus expertes tum numero, tum ordine epistolarum plerumque dissentiunt.
chunks.english: For some are more extensive in their epistles, which they either insert within the Isidorian texts or add at the end. We have been able to find thirteen manuscripts, of which five lack the canons of councils and generally disagree both in the number and order of the epistles.
chunks.notes: Inventory of manuscripts consulted by the Ballerinis.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hujusmodi sunt codices Vat. 629, 3788, 3791, 4873, et Vallicellanus D 38. Codices autem cum epistolis atque canonibus, e quibus ne unum quidem reperire contigit P. Coustantio, nobis hi octo praesto fuere, Vat. 630, 631 et 1340; Vat. Ottobonianus 93, Casanatensis A 11, 14; Florentinus PP. Praedicatorum S. Marci 182, Patavinus 100 canonicorum Lateranensium S. Joannis in Viridario, et Venetus bibliothecae ducalis S. Marci in duos tomos divisus, quorum alter signatur n. 168, alter 169.
chunks.english: Such are the manuscripts Vatican six twenty-nine, thirty-seven eighty-eight, thirty-seven ninety-one, forty-eight seventy-three, and Vallicellana D thirty-eight. But manuscripts containing both epistles and canons—of which Father Coustant was unable to find even one—we had eight available: Vatican six thirty, six thirty-one, and thirteen forty; Vatican Ottobonian ninety-three; Casanatensis A eleven and fourteen; the Florentine manuscript of the Dominicans of San Marco, number one eighty-two; the Paduan manuscript one hundred of the Lateran Canons of San Giovanni in Viridario; and the Venetian manuscript of the Ducal Library of San Marco, divided into two volumes numbered one sixty-eight and one sixty-nine.
chunks.notes: Complete inventory showing the Ballerinis had access to MSS that earlier scholars lacked.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ex his tamen nonnulli mutili, aliquibus epistolis initio vel in fine carent.
chunks.english: Of these, however, some are incomplete, lacking certain epistles at the beginning or end.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: 16. Ut in tanta manuscriptorum varietate discerni possit, quaenam vere fuerit Pseudo-Isidori collectio, seu quae documenta in primaevo ejus fetu indita fuerint, lucem praeferent haec praefationis ejus verba, quibus suae collectionis imaginem ac documentorum summam atque ordinem exprimit.
chunks.english: Section sixteen. So that amid such great variety of manuscripts one may discern what truly was the Pseudo-Isidorian collection, or what documents were included in its original form, light will be shed by these words from his preface, in which he describes the outline and summary and order of the documents in his collection.
chunks.notes: Using Pseudo-Isidore's own preface as a control for reconstructing original contents.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In principio vero voluminis hujus, qualiter concilium apud nos celebretur posuimus, ut qui nostrum ordinem sequi voluerint, sciant qualiter hoc agere debeant. Qui autem hoc agere melius elegerint, faciant quod justo canonicoque atque sapientissimo consilio judicaverint.
chunks.english: The preface states: 'At the beginning of this volume, we have set forth how a council should be celebrated among us, so that those who wish to follow our order may know how this ought to be done. But those who choose to do this in a better way, let them do what they judge by just, canonical, and most wise counsel.'
chunks.notes: Quoting Pseudo-Isidore's preface directly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Denique propter eorum auctoritatem caeteris conciliis praeposuimus canones qui dicuntur Apostolorum, licet a quibusdam apocryphi dicantur, quoniam plures eos recipiunt, et sancti Patres eorum sententias synodali auctoritate roboraverunt, et inter canonicas posuerunt constitutiones.
chunks.english: The preface continues: 'Moreover, because of their authority we have placed before the other councils the canons which are called Apostolic, although they are said by some to be apocryphal, since many receive them, and the holy Fathers have strengthened their decrees by synodal authority and have placed them among the canonical constitutions.'
chunks.notes: Pseudo-Isidore acknowledging disputed status of Apostolic Canons but including them anyway.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Deinde quarumdam epistolarum decreta apostolicorum interseruimus, id est Clementis, Anacleti, Evaristi, et caeterorum apostolicorum quas potuimus hactenus reperire epistolas, usque ad Silvestrum papam.
chunks.english: The preface continues: 'Then we have inserted the decrees from certain letters of the apostolic men, namely those of Clement, Anacletus, Evaristus, and of the other apostolic fathers whose letters we have been able to find up to this point, down to Pope Sylvester.'
chunks.notes: Pseudo-Isidore describing first section: early papal letters (mostly forged).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postmodum vero Nicaenam synodum constituimus propter auctoritatem ejusdem magni concilii. Deinceps diversa concilia Graecorum et Latinorum, sive quae antea, seu quae postmodum facta sunt, sub ordine numerorum ac temporum capitulis suis distincta, sub hujus voluminis aspectu locavimus:
chunks.english: The preface continues: 'Afterward we have placed the Nicene synod because of the authority of that great council. Then we have arranged the various councils of Greeks and Latins, whether those held before or those held afterward, distinguished by their chapters and arranged under numbers and chronological order, within the scope of this volume.'
chunks.notes: Second section: councils, starting with Nicaea.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: subjicientes etiam reliqua decreta praesulum Romanorum usque ad S. Gregorium, et quasdam epistolas ipsius, in quibus pro culmine sedis apostolicae [CCXXVI] non impar conciliorum exstat auctoritas.
chunks.english: The preface concludes: 'We have also added the remaining decrees of the Roman bishops down to Saint Gregory, and certain letters of his, in which the authority is not inferior to that of councils, given the eminence of the apostolic see.'
chunks.notes: Third section: later papal decretals through Gregory the Great.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Illi igitur codices, qui primo post praefationem exhibent ordinem celebrandi concilii, dein canones proferunt apostolorum, postea epistolas apostolicorum, seu priscorum pontificum a S. Clemente ad S. Silvestrum; deinde Nicaenum cum caeteris Graecorum et Latinorum conciliis, ac tandem epistolas alias consequentium pontificum usque ad S. Gregorium; hi, inquam, codices veram et integram Isidorianam collectionem continere dicendi sunt.
chunks.english: Therefore, those manuscripts which first, after the preface, present the order of celebrating a council, then bring forth the canons of the apostles, then the letters of the apostolic men or early pontiffs from Saint Clement to Saint Sylvester, then Nicaea with the other Greek and Latin councils, and finally the other letters of subsequent pontiffs down to Saint Gregory—these manuscripts, I say, are to be considered as containing the true and complete Isidorian collection.
chunks.notes: Establishing criteria for identifying authentic, complete manuscripts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tales autem omnino esse deprehendimus duos [Col. 0252A] codices Vat. 630 et 631, qui sunt plane integri.
chunks.english: We have found that two manuscripts are completely of this type: Vatican six thirty and six thirty-one, which are entirely complete.
chunks.notes: Only two complete MSS identified.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tres autem Vat. Ottobon. 93, Florentinus S. Marci, et Casanatensis, licet tum decretales epistolas, tum conciliorum canones praeferant; cum tamen mutili sint, integrae collectionis notitiam praestare non possunt.
chunks.english: Three others—Vatican Ottobonian ninety-three, the Florentine San Marco manuscript, and the Casanatensis—although they present both the decretal epistles and the canons of councils, are nevertheless incomplete and cannot provide knowledge of the entire collection.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Alius autem Vat. 1340 ac duo Veneti S. Marci plura documenta addunt post S. Gregorium, quae cum Pseudo-Isidoro attribui non possint, ad additamenta referenda sunt de quibus capite sequenti erit sermo.
chunks.english: Another manuscript, Vatican thirteen forty, and the two Venetian San Marco manuscripts, add many documents after Saint Gregory which cannot be attributed to Pseudo-Isidore and must be classified as later additions, about which there will be discussion in the following chapter.
chunks.notes: Distinguishing original contents from later interpolations.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Codicem Patavinum 100 S. Joannis in Viridario, etsi integrum, at posterioris aetatis, diligenter evolvere per tempus non licuit.
chunks.english: The Paduan manuscript one hundred of San Giovanni in Viridario, although complete but of a later age, we did not have time to examine thoroughly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Itaque ex illis duobus integris et puris Isidori codicibus Vat. 630 et 631, quos diligentius expendimus, totius collectionis Isidorianae descriptionem subjiciemus: de aliis autem aliqua addemus postea.
chunks.english: Therefore, from those two complete and pure Isidorian manuscripts, Vatican six thirty and six thirty-one, which we examined more thoroughly, we shall present below a description of the entire Isidorian collection. Concerning the others, we shall add some remarks afterward.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Inter hos autem duos Vaticanos integros codices in serie et ordine praecipue sequemur codicem 630. Is enim praeferendus omnino est omnibus, quippe qui non solum est Gallicanus, id est ejus regionis in qua haec collectio concinnata fuit; verum [Col. 0252B] etiam uti antiquissimus omnium laudatur a Montfauconio et cardinali de Aguirre.
chunks.english: Between these two complete Vatican manuscripts, in the sequence and order we shall chiefly follow manuscript six thirty. For this one must certainly be preferred to all others, since it is not only Gallic—that is, from the very region in which this collection was compiled—but is also praised by Montfaucon and Cardinal de Aguirre as the most ancient of all.
chunks.notes: VAT 630 is both geographically and chronologically closest to the original.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Gallicanum duo probant: 1 o notatio recentiori charactere initio scripta: Hic liber spectat ad usum Joannis episcopi Attrebatensis; 2 o epistola Luitardi Venciensis ad Wenilonem Rothomagensem.
chunks.english: Two things prove it is Gallic: first, the notation written at the beginning in a later hand: 'This book belongs to the use of John, bishop of Arras'; second, the letter of Luitard of Vienne to Wenilo of Rouen.
chunks.notes: Provenance markers in the manuscript.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Antiquissimum vero et ipsi Pseudo-Isidoro supparem declarat catalogus Romanorum pontificum, qui in Leone IV, Benedicto III, et Nicolao I, desinit: ex quo hic codex sub Nicolao I exaratus agnoscitur, id est inter annum 858 et 867, quo quidem tempore documenta hujus collectionis ab Hincmaro et aliis Gallis episcopis allegari coeperunt.
chunks.english: But that it is most ancient and contemporary with Pseudo-Isidore himself is shown by the catalog of Roman pontiffs, which ends with Leo the Fourth, Benedict the Third, and Nicholas the First. From this it is recognized that this manuscript was written under Nicholas the First, that is, between the years eight fifty-eight and eight sixty-seven—precisely the time when documents from this collection began to be cited by Hincmar and other Gallic bishops.
chunks.notes: Dating VAT 630 to the 860s based on papal catalog.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc idem codex unum e primis ejusdem collectionis exemplaribus habendus: quod ipsum etiam atque etiam confirmant duae appendiculae quorumdam documentorum, quae cum posterioribus curis Pseudo-Isidorus addidisset, in hoc primigeniae collectionis exemplo adjectae sunt in fine; cum in aliis codicibus suo loco insertae inveniantur.
chunks.english: Therefore, this same manuscript should be considered one of the first exemplars of the same collection. This is confirmed again and again by two appendices of certain documents which Pseudo-Isidore added in later revisions; in this example of the original collection they were added at the end, whereas in other manuscripts they are found inserted in their proper places.
chunks.notes: VAT 630 represents an earlier edition with later additions appended rather than integrated.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Huic ergo codici in [Col. 0252C] constituenda ac describenda eadem collectione quantum prae caeteris sit deferendum, nemo non videt.
chunks.english: Therefore, everyone can see how much deference should be given to this manuscript above the others in establishing and describing the same collection.
chunks.notes: Methodological statement: VAT 630 is the base text.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: 17. Ante collectionem praeter catalogum Romanorum pontificum, quem in Nicolao 1 desinere monuimus, affertur annotatio de quatuor conciliis generalibus. Haec legitur apud Gratianum dist. 15, c. 1, ac ex S. Isidoro Hispalensi lib. VI Originum, c. 16, sumpta est.
chunks.english: Section seventeen. Before the collection itself, besides the catalog of Roman pontiffs which we noted ends with Nicholas the First, there is a note about the four general councils. This is found in Gratian's Decretum, distinction fifteen, canon one, and is taken from Saint Isidore of Seville's Origins, book six, chapter sixteen.
chunks.notes: Preliminary materials in VAT 630 before Pseudo-Isidore's preface begins.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dein sequitur annotatio de sex conciliis, uti apud eumdem Gratianum dist. 16, c. 10, tum alia annotatio de aliis conciliis uno et viginti, quorum octo priora sunt Ancyranum, Neocaesariense, Gangrense, Sardicense, Antiochenum, Laodicenum, Carthaginense et Africanum, quae Hadrianae collectionis seriem referunt; caetera vero tredecim sunt Gallicana, nullum Hispanicum: quod hanc annotationem in Galliis conditam indicat.
chunks.english: Then follows a note about six councils, as in the same Gratian, distinction sixteen, canon ten. Then another note about twenty-one other councils, of which the first eight are Ancyra, Neocaesarea, Gangra, Sardica, Antioch, Laodicea, Carthage, and the African council, which follow the series of the Hadrianic collection. But the other thirteen are Gallic, with none Spanish—which indicates that this note was composed in Gaul.
chunks.notes: Conciliar lists showing Gallic rather than Spanish orientation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Eadem apud Gratianum exstat dist. 16, c. 11, ubi tamen tria alia Gallicana concilia adduntur, quae idcirco complent numerum 24.
chunks.english: The same list exists in Gratian, distinction sixteen, canon eleven, where however three other Gallic councils are added, thus completing the number to twenty-four.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tum in codice post mentionem concilii Maticensis [Col. 0250D] propositam numero 21 subjiciuntur alii octo numeri a 22 usque ad 29, qui tamen vacui relinquuntur.
chunks.english: Then in the manuscript, after the mention of the Council of Mâcon proposed as number twenty-one, eight other numbers are added from twenty-two to twenty-nine, which however are left empty.
chunks.notes: Blank entries suggesting planned but unexecuted expansion.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sequuntur porro alii decem et octo numeri a 30 ad 47, in quibus indicantur concilia Hispanica, et reliqua, quae in tabula conciliorum dein recelenda iisdem numeris referuntur.
chunks.english: Then follow another eighteen numbers from thirty to forty-seven, in which are indicated the Hispanic councils and others, which are listed with the same numbers in the table of councils to be recited shortly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Subjicitur notitia provinciarum, quam Andreas Scotus, Petrus Bertius, et Emmanuel Schelestratius publici juris fecerunt; ac postea notitia Galliarum, quae a pluribus vulgata est.
chunks.english: There is appended a notice of provinces, which Andreas Scotus, Peter Bertius, and Emmanuel Schelestratius have made public; and afterward a notice of the Gauls, which has been published by many.
chunks.notes: Standard reference materials copied into the MS.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hae omnes annotationes atque notitiae non solum exstant similiter in ms. Vat. 631 aliisque exemplis quae Isidorianam integram collectionem continent, verum etiam in codice Vat. 1341 collectionis Hispanicae, originis Gallicanae, ita ut dubitandum non sit quin vel ex aliquo ejusmodi exemplo collectionis Hispanicae traductae fuerint in manuscripta Isidori, vel ex mss. Isidorianis in eos codices collectionis Hispanicae transierint.
chunks.english: All these annotations and notices exist not only similarly in Vatican manuscript six thirty-one and other examples that contain the complete Isidorian collection, but also in Vatican manuscript thirteen forty-one of the Hispanic collection of Gallic origin. Therefore, there can be no doubt that either they were transferred from some such example of the Hispanic collection into Isidorian manuscripts, or from Isidorian manuscripts into those codices of the Hispanic collection.
chunks.notes: Cross-contamination between Hispanic and Pseudo-Isidorian manuscript traditions.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec autem omnia quae praecedunt [CCXXVII] et non pertinent ad collectionem Isidori, claudit memorata epistola Luitardi episcopi Venciensis ad Wenilonem Rothomagensem, scripta an. 868, quae solius manuscripti Vat. 630 propria, ex eodem edita est tom. X Conciliorum, edit. Ven. col. 1403.
chunks.english: But all these things that precede and do not pertain to Isidore's collection are closed by the aforementioned letter of Luitard, bishop of Vienne, to Wenilo of Rouen, written in the year eight sixty-eight, which is unique to Vatican manuscript six thirty and was published from it in volume ten of the Councils, Venetian edition, column fourteen hundred three.
chunks.notes: Additional dating evidence from unique content in VAT 630.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: 18. Post haec collectio Isidoriana exorditur a praefatione hoc titulo: Incipit praefatio S. Isidori episcopi. Isidorus Mercator servus Christi lectori conservo suo et parenti in Domino fidei salutem.
chunks.english: Section eighteen. After these preliminary matters, the Isidorian collection begins with the preface under this title: 'Here begins the preface of Saint Isidore the bishop. Isidore Mercator, servant of Christ, to his fellow-servant the reader and parent in the Lord: greetings in the faith.'
chunks.notes: Beginning of Pseudo-Isidore's actual work. 'Mercator' is significant variant.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Compellor a multis, etc. Edita est ex codice Vat. 630 a cardinali de Aguirre. Vide tomum I Concil., pag. 4. Merlinus omittit Mercator, et pro fidei habet fideli.
chunks.english: It continues: 'I am compelled by many,' and so forth. It was published from Vatican manuscript six thirty by Cardinal de Aguirre. See volume one of the Councils, page four. Merlin omits 'Mercator' and has 'faithful one' instead of 'in the faith.'
chunks.notes: Textual variants in the preface itself.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vulgati Conciliorum pro Mercator substituunt peccator, quod nulli nostri codices praeferunt. Mercator quoque legitur in codice Parisiensi quem vidit Harduinus, nec non apud Ivonem in decreto; et solum in hujus margine peccator.
chunks.english: The common editions of the Councils substitute 'sinner' for 'Mercator,' which none of our manuscripts present. 'Mercator' is also read in the Paris manuscript which Harduin saw, as well as in Ivo's Decretum; and only in the margin of Ivo's work appears 'sinner.'
chunks.notes: 'Mercator' is the older reading; 'peccator' a later substitution.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praefationi subjicitur epistola supposititia Aurelii ad Damasum, cujus initium est: Gloriam apostolicae sedis, nec non apocrypha Damasi responsio, Scripta [Col. 0253B] sanctitatis tuae, etc., quas videsis tom. II Concil., col. 1011 et 1012.
chunks.english: To the preface is added the spurious letter of Aurelius to Damasus, which begins 'The glory of the apostolic see,' as well as the apocryphal response of Damasus, 'The writings of your holiness,' and so forth. See these in volume two of the Councils, columns ten eleven and ten twelve.
chunks.notes: First forged documents after the preface.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In ms. Vat. 631 hae describuntur ante praefationem, sed perperam; nam Hincmarus Remensis in opusculo adversus Hincmarum Laudunensem c. 24, Damasi responsionem ad Aurelium laudans, quam, inquit, Isidorus suae praefationi supposuit, et collectioni praeposuit.
chunks.english: In Vatican manuscript six thirty-one these are written before the preface, but incorrectly. For Hincmar of Reims, in his work against Hincmar of Laon, chapter twenty-four, when citing the response of Damasus to Aurelius, says that 'Isidore placed it under his preface and placed it before his collection.'
chunks.notes: Using Hincmar as a ninth-century witness to the structure of the collection.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postea sequitur Ordo de celebrando concilio. Hora diei prima, etc., uti tom. I Concil. a col., 8 usque ad col. 12. Hunc quidem ordinem, qui sumptus est ex concilio Toletano IV, initio collectionis praemittendum auctor in praefatione receperat.
chunks.english: Afterward follows the Order for Celebrating a Council: 'At the first hour of the day,' and so forth, as in volume one of the Councils from column eight to column twelve. This order, which was taken from the Fourth Council of Toledo, the author had promised in his preface to place at the beginning of his collection.
chunks.notes: Fulfilling promise made in preface; content borrowed from Toledo IV.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dein affertur tabula conciliorum cum titulis 47, quorum primi 45 illi sunt quos in Hispanica exhibuimus c. 4, n. 5; alii autem duo sunt hi: XLVI. Decreta quorumdam praesulum Romanorum ad fidei regulam et disciplinam ecclesiasticam constituta. XLVII. Synodus Toletana 68 episcoporum.
chunks.english: Then is presented a table of councils with forty-seven titles, of which the first forty-five are those which we displayed in the Hispanic collection in chapter four, number five. But the other two are these: Forty-six, Decrees of certain Roman bishops established for the rule of faith and ecclesiastical discipline. Forty-seven, Synod of Toledo with sixty-eight bishops.
chunks.notes: List of councils to be included; partially matches Hispanic collection.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec eadem tabula cum iisdem titulis 47 exstat etiam in codice Casanatensi A 11, nec non in Vat. 1341 continente collectionem Hispanicam originis Gallicanae; ac propterea ex alterutro fonte in alterum irrepsit.
chunks.english: This same table with the same forty-seven titles also exists in the Casanatensis manuscript A eleven, as well as in Vatican thirteen forty-one containing the Hispanic collection of Gallic origin; and therefore it crept from one source into the other.
chunks.notes: Cross-contamination between collections.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Certe duo tituli 46 et 47, qui nec cum Hispanicae, nec cum Isidorianae collectionis corpore concordant, addititii noscuntur. Error quidem inest in titulo 46, qui non exprimit ullum concilium, sed est ille ipse titulus in Hispanica collectione praefixus parti secundae, quae decretales sedis apostolicae continet.
chunks.english: Certainly the two titles forty-six and forty-seven, which agree neither with the body of the Hispanic nor with that of the Isidorian collection, are recognized as additions. Indeed there is an error in title forty-six, which does not express any council but is that very title in the Hispanic collection prefixed to the second part, which contains the decretals of the apostolic see.
chunks.notes: Identifying errors in the table of contents.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Subduntur aliae duae epistolae supposititiae, altera S. Hieronymi ad Damasum, cujus initium: Gloriam sanctitatis tuae, etc.; altera Damasi ad Hieronymum, quae incipit: Gaudet Ecclesia tuo fonte, etc.
chunks.english: There are added two other spurious letters: one of Saint Jerome to Damasus, which begins 'The glory of your holiness,' and so forth; the other of Damasus to Jerome, which begins 'The Church rejoices in your fountain,' and so forth.
chunks.notes: More forged correspondence to boost papal authority.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Editae sunt ab Henschenio, Schelestratio et aliis; novissime autem insertae sunt Appendici Operum S. Hieronymi tom. XI editionis Veronensis pag. 275.
chunks.english: These were published by Henschenius, Schelestratius, and others; most recently they were inserted in the Appendix to the Works of Saint Jerome, volume eleven of the Veronese edition, page two seventy-five.
chunks.notes: Bibliographic references for these spurious texts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum hae inveniantur etiam in quibusdam mss. pontificalis Romani Damaso inscripti quod Pseudo-Isidorus adhibuit, num harum suppositio ipsum Pseudo-Isidorum [Col. 0253D] an alium antiquiorem habeat auctorem ex quo ipse illas receperit, definire non licet.
chunks.english: Since these are also found in certain manuscripts of the Roman Pontifical ascribed to Damasus which Pseudo-Isidore used, it cannot be determined whether the forgery of these letters had Pseudo-Isidore himself as author, or some earlier forger from whom he received them.
chunks.notes: Uncertainty about whether Pseudo-Isidore forged these or copied existing forgeries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Mox ex collectione Hadriana describuntur quinquaginta canones apostolorum versionis Dionysianae.
chunks.english: Next, the fifty canons of the apostles in the Dionysian version are copied from the Hadrianic collection.
chunks.notes: Authentic material: the Apostolic Canons as received in the West.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Forged Letters of the Early Popes
chunks.latin: Tum sequuntur epistolae priscorum pontificum a S. Clemente ad Melchiadem usque, omnes supposititiae, et omnes a Pseudo-Isidoro confictae, duabus tantummodo S. Clementis exceptis, quas tamen ille interpolavit. En ipsarum seriem, quas in editis Conciliorum invenire licebit.
chunks.english: Then follow the letters of the early pontiffs from Saint Clement to Miltiades, all spurious and all fabricated by Pseudo-Isidore, with the exception of only two of Saint Clement, which however he interpolated. Here is their series, which may be found in the published editions of the Councils.
chunks.notes: Beginning the catalog of forged papal letters—the heart of the Pseudo-Isidorian fraud.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Epistolae quinque S. Clementis, duae ad Jacobum, tertia ad omnes episcopos; presbyteros, etc.; quarta ad Julium et Julianum, quinta ad Hierosolymis habitantes cum Jacobo episcopo.
chunks.english: Five letters of Saint Clement: two to James, the third to all bishops and presbyters and so forth, the fourth to Julius and Julian, the fifth to those dwelling in Jerusalem with Bishop James.
chunks.notes: Clement I (pope c.88-97): only 2 genuine, rest forged, all interpolated.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Duae primae jam a Graeco aliquo confictae fuerunt; et a Rufino Latinitate donatae, in vetustiores collectiones tum Italicas, tum Gallicanas transierant.
chunks.english: The first two had already been fabricated by some Greek author and translated into Latin by Rufinus, and had passed into older Italian and Gallic collections.
chunks.notes: Pseudo-Clementine literature predates Pseudo-Isidore.

chunks.speaker: narrator
