chunks.latin: Solum epistola secunda in collectionibus Italicis inscribitur: Praecepta S. Petri, quo titulo illam ex iisdem collectionibus hoc tomo [Col. 0254A] edemus inter documenta juris canonici veteris; at in Gallicanis codicibus ab Harduino et Coustantio indicatis inscribitur: Epistola praeceptorum S. Clementis papae missa Jacobo fratri Domini, vel Epistola S. Clementis Romani ad Jacobum de sacramentis Ecclesiae.
chunks.english: Only the second letter is inscribed in Italian collections as 'The Precepts of Saint Peter,' under which title we shall publish it in this volume among the documents of ancient canon law. But in the Gallic manuscripts indicated by Hardouin and Coustant it is inscribed: 'Letter of the precepts of Saint Clement the pope sent to James the brother of the Lord,' or 'Letter of Saint Clement of Rome to James concerning the sacraments of the Church.'
chunks.notes: Different titles in Italian vs. Gallic manuscript traditions.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Has duas epistolas Pseudo-Isidorus ex Gallicanis collectionibus excerpsit, unde in secunda epistolae inscriptionem retinuit. Tres vero alias epistolas ipse supposuit.
chunks.english: Pseudo-Isidore excerpted these two letters from Gallic collections, from which he retained the inscription in the second letter. But the three other letters he forged himself.
chunks.notes: Distinguishing what Pseudo-Isidore copied from what he invented.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Illis autem duabus spuriis epistolis quas aliunde accepit, novas imposturas addidit cum eas prolixis interpolationibus auxit. Has interpolationes quisque dignoscet si easdem epistolas ex Isidoriana collectione in Conciliis editas conferat cum illis multo brevioribus quae ex versione Rufiniana et ex antiquioribus collectionibus hoc ipso tomo proferuntur, prima in collectione subjicienda cap. 64, col. 454, altera inter documenta paulo ante laudata num. 5, col. 674.
chunks.english: But to those two spurious letters which he received from elsewhere, he added new forgeries when he expanded them with lengthy interpolations. Anyone can recognize these interpolations if they compare the same letters as published from the Isidorian collection in the Councils with those much shorter versions that are brought forth from Rufinus' translation and from older collections in this very volume: the first in the collection to be presented in chapter sixty-four, column four fifty-four; the second among the documents cited a little earlier, number five, column six seventy-four.
chunks.notes: Method for detecting Pseudo-Isidore's additions: compare with pre-existing versions.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: S. Anacleti epistolae tres, prima ad omnes Orientales, secunda ad episcopos Italiae, tertia ad episcopos omnes. S. Evaristi duae, una ad Africanos episcopos, altera ad Aegyptios. S. Alexandri I epistolae tres, prior ad omnes orthodoxos, aliae duae ad episcopos omnes.
chunks.english: Three letters of Saint Anacletus: the first to all Easterners, the second to the bishops of Italy, the third to all bishops. Two of Saint Evaristus: one to the bishops of Africa, the other to the Egyptians. Three letters of Saint Alexander the First: the first to all the orthodox, the other two to all bishops.
chunks.notes: Continuing catalog of forged early papal letters (late 1st/early 2nd century popes).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: S. Xysti I epistolae duae, prima ad omnes Christianos, altera ad omnes episcopos. S. Thelesphori una ad omnes Christianos. S. Hygini epistolae duae, una ad omnes Christianos, altera ad Athenienses.
chunks.english: Two letters of Saint Sixtus the First: the first to all Christians, the second to all bishops. One of Saint Telesphorus to all Christians. Two letters of Saint Hyginus: one to all Christians, the other to the Athenians.
chunks.notes: Early 2nd-century popes (c.115-140).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: S. Pii I item duae, prima ad omnes Christianos, ad Italos altera. S. Anitii (pro Aniceti) una ad episcopos Galliae. S. Soteris duae, prior ad Campaniae, secunda ad Italiae episcopos.
chunks.english: Likewise two of Saint Pius the First: the first to all Christians, the second to the Italians. One of Saint Anitius (for Anicetus) to the bishops of Gaul. Two of Saint Soter: the first to the bishops of Campania, the second to the bishops of Italy.
chunks.notes: Mid-2nd-century popes (c.140-175). Note variant 'Anitius' for Anicetus.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: S. Eleutherii una ad Galliae provincias. S. Victoris I epistolae duae, una ad Theophilum episcopum Alexandrinum, alia ad Africanos. S. Zephirini duae, prima ad Siciliae, altera ad [Col. 0254C] Aegypti episcopos.
chunks.english: One of Saint Eleutherius to the provinces of Gaul. Two letters of Saint Victor the First: one to Theophilus, bishop of Alexandria, the other to the Africans. Two of Saint Zephyrinus: the first to the bishops of Sicily, the second to the bishops of Egypt.
chunks.notes: Late 2nd/early 3rd-century popes (c.175-217).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: S. Callisti I duae, una ad Benedictum episcopum, alia ad episcopos Gallos. S. Urbani I una ad omnes Christianos. S. Pontiani duae, prior ad Felicem Succribonum, altera ad Christianos omnes.
chunks.english: Two of Saint Callistus the First: one to Bishop Benedict, the other to the Gallic bishops. One of Saint Urban the First to all Christians. Two of Saint Pontian: the first to Felix of Succribonum, the second to all Christians.
chunks.notes: Early 3rd-century popes (c.217-235).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: S. Anteri una ad episcopos provinciarum Baeticae et Toletanae. S. Fabiani tres, prima ad omnes episcopos, secunda ad Orientales, tertia ad Hilarium episcopum. S. Cornelii duae, una ad omnes episcopos, ad Rusticum episcopum altera.
chunks.english: One of Saint Anterus to the bishops of the provinces of Baetica and Toledo. Three of Saint Fabian: the first to all bishops, the second to the Easterners, the third to Bishop Hilary. Two of Saint Cornelius: one to all bishops, the other to Bishop Rusticus.
chunks.notes: Mid-3rd-century popes (c.235-253). Fabian is important in dating discussions.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: S. Lucii I una ad episcopos Galliae et Hispaniae. S. Stephani I duae, prior ad Hilarium episcopum, secunda ad episcopos omnes. S. Xysti II duae, prior ad Gratum episcopum, secunda ad episcopos Hispaniae.
chunks.english: One of Saint Lucius the First to the bishops of Gaul and Spain. Two of Saint Stephen the First: the first to Bishop Hilary, the second to all bishops. Two of Saint Sixtus the Second: the first to Bishop Gratus, the second to the bishops of Spain.
chunks.notes: Mid-3rd-century popes (c.253-258).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: S. Dionysii duae, una ad Urbanum praefectum, altera ad Severum episcopum. [Col. 0254D] S. Felicis I epistolae tres, prima ad Paternum episcopum, secunda ad episcopos Galliae, tertia ad Benignum episcopum.
chunks.english: Two of Saint Dionysius: one to Urban the prefect, the other to Bishop Severus. Three letters of Saint Felix the First: the first to Bishop Paternus, the second to the bishops of Gaul, the third to Bishop Benignus.
chunks.notes: Late 3rd-century popes (c.259-274).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: S. Eutychiani duae, prior ad Baeticae, secunda ad Siciliae episcopos. S. Gaii una ad Felicem episcopum. S. Marcellini duae, ad Salomonem episcopum una, altera ad episcopos Orientis.
chunks.english: Two of Saint Eutychian: the first to the bishops of Baetica, the second to the bishops of Sicily. One of Saint Gaius to Bishop Felix. Two of Saint Marcellinus: one to Bishop Solomon, the other to the bishops of the East.
chunks.notes: Late 3rd/early 4th-century popes (c.275-304).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: S. Marcelli duae, prima ad episcopos Antiochenae provinciae, secunda ad Maxentium tyrannum. S. Eusebii tres, prior ad Galliae, altera ad Aegypti, tertia ad Tusciae et Campaniae episcopos.
chunks.english: Two of Saint Marcellus: the first to the bishops of the province of Antioch, the second to the tyrant Maxentius. Three of Saint Eusebius: the first to the bishops of Gaul, the second to those of Egypt, the third to the bishops of Tuscany and Campania.
chunks.notes: Early 4th-century popes (c.308-311).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: S. Melchiadis una ad episcopos Hispaniae. In hac prima parte, quae ad priscos pontifices pertinet a S. Clemente usque ad Melchiadem inclusive, seu exclusive usque ad S. Silvestrum, Pseudo-Isidorus quinquaginta novem epistolas finxit, et duas S. Clementi olim affictas retulit ac interpolavit.
chunks.english: One of Saint Miltiades to the bishops of Spain. In this first part, which pertains to the early pontiffs from Saint Clement to Miltiades inclusive (or exclusively, up to Saint Sylvester), Pseudo-Isidore forged fifty-nine letters, and he included and interpolated two that had been previously attributed to Saint Clement.
chunks.notes: Summary: 59 forgeries + 2 interpolated pseudo-Clementines = 61 spurious papal letters before Constantine.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Conciliar Section and Its Spurious Introductions
chunks.latin: 19. Sequitur ea pars quae concilia exhibet. Praemittitur [Col. 0255A] opusculum inscriptum de Primitiva Ecclesia et Synodo Nicaena.
chunks.english: Section nineteen. The part that presents the councils follows. There is prefaced a work entitled 'On the Primitive Church and the Nicene Synod.'
chunks.notes: Transitioning to the conciliar section of the collection.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hoc opusculum cum subjiciatur litteris Melchiadis, ejusdem pontificis nomine inscriptum a Gratiano aliisque allegatur. Sed in codicibus nomine caret. Est autem veluti prooemium ipsius Pseudo-Isidori in constitutionem sequentem. Incipit: Nemo qui divinas Scripturas legit, etc., uti tom. I Concil., Ven. edit. col. 1562.
chunks.english: Since this work is placed after the letters of Miltiades, it is cited by Gratian and others under the name of that same pontiff. But in the manuscripts it lacks a name. It is, however, like a preface by Pseudo-Isidore himself to the following constitution. It begins: 'No one who reads the divine Scriptures,' and so forth, as in volume one of the Councils, Venetian edition, column fifteen sixty-two.
chunks.notes: Anonymous in MSS but later attributed to Miltiades.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Subjicitur Constitutio Constantini ad Silvestrum. In nomine, etc. Ea quae Salvator et Redemptor noster, etc. Ibidem, col. 1564. Apocryphum est hoc documentum.
chunks.english: There is added the Constitution of Constantine to Sylvester: 'In the name,' and so forth. 'Those things which our Savior and Redeemer,' and so forth. In the same place, column fifteen sixty-four. This document is apocryphal.
chunks.notes: The 'Donation of Constantine'—famous medieval forgery.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quidam istud Pseudo-Isidoro tribuunt. Balsamon quaedam Graeca ejus fragmenta recitat. Integrum autem documentum Graece scriptum invenit P. Possinus in Vaticanis codicibus Graecis 614 fol. 76, 789 fol. 185, 973 fol. 39, et 1416 fol. 127, uti P. Labbeus notavit.
chunks.english: Some attribute this to Pseudo-Isidore. Balsamon recites certain Greek fragments of it. But Father Possin found the complete document written in Greek in Vatican Greek manuscripts six fourteen, folio seventy-six; seven eighty-nine, folio one eighty-five; nine seventy-three, folio thirty-nine; and fourteen sixteen, folio one twenty-seven, as Father Labbé noted.
chunks.notes: Donation exists in Greek, so may predate Pseudo-Isidore.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc non improbabiliter suspicati sunt Baronius et Binius hoc constitutum a Graecis fuisse confictum, et postea Latine redditum, atque a Pseudo-Isidoro exceptum.
chunks.english: From this, Baronius and Binius not improbably suspected that this constitution was fabricated by Greeks, and afterward rendered into Latin and taken up by Pseudo-Isidore.
chunks.notes: Scholarly consensus: Donation is Greek forgery adopted by Pseudo-Isidore.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Illud [Col. 0255B] quidem legitur Latine in collectione Gallicana, quam primo loco exhibet vetustus codex Colbertinus 3368. Vide quae diximus part. II, c. 8, n. 3.
chunks.english: Indeed, it is found in Latin in the Gallic collection which the ancient Colbert manuscript thirty-three sixty-eight presents in first place. See what we said in part two, chapter eight, number three.
chunks.notes: Donation circulated in Gaul before Pseudo-Isidore.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Etsi vero hoc documentum ibidem addititium videatur; cum tamen eadem collectio Colbertina sit Pseudo-Isidoro antiquior, nec quidquam Isidorianum receperit; ipsum ex hac, vel ex alia fortassis vetustiore Gallicana collectione hoc monumentum ebibisse satis credibile est.
chunks.english: Although this document seems to be an addition in that collection, nevertheless since the same Colbert collection is older than Pseudo-Isidore and has received nothing Isidorian, it is quite credible that he drank this document from this source, or perhaps from another older Gallic collection.
chunks.notes: Pseudo-Isidore's source for the Donation identified.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sequitur: Quo tempore Nicaenum concilium habitum sit. Canones generalium conciliorum a temporibus Constantini coeperunt, etc., ac desinit in verbis secundum post apostolos symbolum tradiderunt. Est initium praefationis collectionis Hispanicae, quam integram dedimus cap. 4, n. 5.
chunks.english: There follows: 'At what time the Nicene council was held. The canons of general councils began from the times of Constantine,' and so forth, and it ends with the words 'they handed down the second creed after the apostles.' This is the beginning of the preface of the Hispanic collection, which we gave in full in chapter four, number five.
chunks.notes: Borrowing the Hispanic collection's preface to introduce Nicaea.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Incipit epistola vel praefatio Nicaeni concilii. Beatissimo Silvestro in urbe Roma apostolicae sedis antistite, etc. Est praefatio a Pseudo-Isidoro sumpta ex collectione hoc tomo edita. Vide col. 22.
chunks.english: There begins the letter or preface of the Nicene council: 'To the most blessed Sylvester, bishop of the apostolic see in the city of Rome,' and so forth. This is the preface taken by Pseudo-Isidore from the collection published in this volume. See column twenty-two.
chunks.notes: Using material from another Gallic collection.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postea describuntur canones conciliorum Graeciae, Nicaeni, Ancyrani, Neocaesariensis, Gangrensis, Sardicensis, Antiocheni, Laodiceni, Constantinopolitani, Ephesini et Chalcedonensis, ac formata Attici.
chunks.english: Afterward are copied the canons of the councils of Greece: Nicaea, Ancyra, Neocaesarea, Gangra, Sardica, Antioch, Laodicea, Constantinople, Ephesus, and Chalcedon, as well as the Formula of Atticus.
chunks.notes: Greek councils in chronological order.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Item canones octo conciliorum Africae, septem Carthaginensium, et Milevitani. Dein canones conciliorum Galliae, trium Arelatensium, Valentini, Tauritani, Regensis, Arausicani, Vasensis, Agathensis, Aurelianensis.
chunks.english: Likewise the canons of eight councils of Africa: seven of Carthage and of Milevis. Then the canons of the councils of Gaul: three of Arles, Valence, Turin, Riez, Orange, Vaison, Agde, and Orléans.
chunks.notes: African and Gallic councils.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tandem canones conciliorum Hispaniae, Eliberitani, Tarraconensis, Gerumdensis, Caesaraugustani, Ilerdensis, Valentini, Toletanorum I, II, III et IV; initium Bracarensis I; quo interrupto inseruntur aliae novem synodi Toletanae a V ad XIII, quae ultima apud Merlinum in fine mutila, in Vat. 630 aliisque Isidorianis integra legitur.
chunks.english: Finally, the canons of the councils of Spain: Elvira, Tarragona, Gerona, Zaragoza, Lérida, Valencia, and the first, second, third, and fourth councils of Toledo; the beginning of Braga One, which is interrupted by the insertion of nine other synods of Toledo from five to thirteen. The last of these, which is incomplete at the end in Merlin's edition, is read complete in Vatican six thirty and other Isidorian manuscripts.
chunks.notes: Spanish councils, including interruption in Braga sequence.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postea iterum subditur synodus Bracarensis I, sed integra; et mox Bracarensis II. Dein capitula Martini Bracarensis, et concilium Bracarense anni 675; ac tandem duae synodi Hispalenses.
chunks.english: Afterward, the synod of Braga One is again added, but complete; and immediately Braga Two. Then the chapters of Martin of Braga, and the Council of Braga of the year six seventy-five; and finally two synods of Seville.
chunks.notes: Repetition of Braga I (earlier truncated) now complete.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec omnia Pseudo-Isidorus sumpsit ex auctiori collectione Hispanica originis Gallicanae, qualis est in ms. Vat. 1341, cum eadem repetitione Bracarensis I, et cum iisdem lectionibus liberius emendatis, uti antea monuimus.
chunks.english: All these things Pseudo-Isidore took from the fuller Hispanic collection of Gallic origin, such as is in Vatican manuscript thirteen forty-one, with the same repetition of Braga One and with the same readings freely emended, as we noted earlier.
chunks.notes: Confirming Gallic source and identifying specific manuscript lineage.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Solum in Nicaeno post symbolum omittitur episcoporum catalogus, qui in eodem codice collectionis Hispanicae legitur.
chunks.english: Only in the Nicene council, after the creed, is the catalog of bishops omitted, which is read in the same manuscript of the Hispanic collection.
chunks.notes: One editorial change Pseudo-Isidore made: omitting the list of Nicene fathers.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Later Papal Decretals: Genuine and Forged
chunks.latin: 20. Tertia pars sequitur, quae epistolas profert caeterorum praesulum Romanorum a S. Silvestro ad S. Gregorium usque. Eadem praefatio his praemittitur, Hactenus digestis conciliis, etc., quam in Hispanica attulimus cap. 4, n. 6.
chunks.english: Section twenty. The third part follows, which presents the letters of the other Roman bishops from Saint Sylvester to Saint Gregory. The same preface is placed before these, 'Having arranged the councils thus far,' and so forth, which we presented in the Hispanic collection in chapter four, number six.
chunks.notes: Third major section: post-Nicene papal letters, mixing genuine and forged.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Primum autem documentum quod producitur supposititium est. Incipiunt excerpta quaedam ex synodalibus gestis S. Silvestri papae. Temporibus S. Silvestri papae et Constantini piissimi Augusti, factum est magnum concilium in Nicaea, etc., uti tom. I Concil., a col. 1575 usque ad verba columnae 1578 designatus sit chirographus.
chunks.english: But the first document that is produced is spurious. There begin certain excerpts from the synodal acts of Saint Sylvester the pope: 'In the times of Saint Sylvester the pope and Constantine the most pious Augustus, a great council was held at Nicaea,' and so forth, as in volume one of the Councils, from column fifteen seventy-five to the words in column fifteen seventy-eight, 'let it be designated in writing.'
chunks.notes: Forged 'Sylvester synod' opening the papal decretals section.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum vero in editione Conciliorum his excerptis subjiciantur ipsa synodalia gesta S. Silvestri cum titulo: Canon, sive Constitutum Silvestri, ex quibus haec excerpta Pseudo-Isidorus decerpsit; haec quoque gesta, quae aeque supposititia sunt, in Isidoriana collectione intexta ibidem traduntur.
chunks.english: But since in the edition of the Councils these excerpts are followed by the synodal acts of Saint Sylvester themselves with the title 'Canon, or Constitution of Sylvester,' from which Pseudo-Isidore plucked these excerpts, these acts too, which are equally spurious, are reported as woven into the Isidorian collection in that place.
chunks.notes: Full 'Sylvester Acts' included in some editions but not in original Pseudo-Isidore.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Verum licet in aliquo codice Isidoriano legantur, absunt tamen a nostro codice et caeteris quos consuluimus. Inanem quidem laborem ille assumpsisset concinnando excerpta ex iisdem gestis, si ipsamet gesta suae collectioni inserenda putasset.
chunks.english: But although they are read in some Isidorian manuscript, they are nevertheless absent from our manuscript and the others we have consulted. Indeed, he would have undertaken useless labor in arranging excerpts from those same acts if he had thought the acts themselves should be inserted into his collection.
chunks.notes: VAT 630 has only excerpts, not full forged Sylvester acts—evidence of earlier recension.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec tantum excerpta in suo quoque codice inventa memorat Hincmarus Remensis in saepe laudato opusculo contra Hincmarum Laudunensem c. 24, ubi etiam gesta ejusmodi veluti apocrypha rejicit.
chunks.english: Hincmar of Reims mentions finding only these excerpts in his manuscript as well, in the often-cited work against Hincmar of Laon, chapter twenty-four, where he also rejects such acts as apocryphal.
chunks.notes: Ninth-century testimony confirming excerpts-only in early MSS.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum porro ea sub Symmachi aetatem una cum aliis documentis apocryphis concinnata, inserta fuerint quibusdam collectionibus Italicis, ac in Gallias etiam transierint, uti leguntur in vetusto ms. Colbertino 784, de quo confer part. II, c. 6, n. 4 et 5, Pseudo-Isidorus ex aliquo ejusmodi Gallicano exemplo haec excerpta deduxisse dicendus est.
chunks.english: Moreover, since they were composed under the time of Symmachus along with other apocryphal documents and were inserted into certain Italian collections and also passed into Gaul (as they are read in the ancient Colbert manuscript seven eighty-four, concerning which see part two, chapter six, numbers four and five), Pseudo-Isidore must be said to have derived these excerpts from some such Gallic exemplar.
chunks.notes: Tracing the Sylvester forgery to earlier Italian/Gallic sources.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Caeterum prae oculis habuisse etiam pontificale Romanum Damasi nomine inscriptum, cujus auctor eadem S. Silvestri synodalia [Col. 0256B] gesta compendio exhibet, nihil dubii est. Quaedam enim particulae excerptorum Pseudo-Isidori cum pontificalis abbreviatione magis quam cum ipsis gestis conveniunt.
chunks.english: Furthermore, there is no doubt that he also had before his eyes the Roman Pontifical inscribed with the name of Damasus, whose author presents the same synodal acts of Saint Sylvester in summary. For certain passages of Pseudo-Isidore's excerpts agree more with the Pontifical's abbreviation than with the acts themselves.
chunks.notes: Pseudo-Isidore used multiple sources, sometimes conflating them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Epistola supposititia Athanasii et caeterorum Aegyptiorum ad Marcum papam, qua petunt exemplum canonum Nicaenorum; et rescriptum Marci ad eosdem item supposititium. Vide tom. II Concil., col. 488 et 490.
chunks.english: The spurious letter of Athanasius and the other Egyptians to Pope Mark, in which they request a copy of the Nicene canons; and the response of Mark to them, likewise spurious. See volume two of the Councils, columns four eighty-eight and four ninety.
chunks.notes: Forged correspondence under Pope Mark (336).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Fides in sacro Romano tractata concilio a beatissimo Julio papa et reliquis verae fidei episcopis. In nomine Domini Dei et Salvatoris, etc. Ibi col. 549. Haec quoque est Pseudo-Isidoriana impostura.
chunks.english: The faith treated in the sacred Roman council by the most blessed Pope Julius and the other bishops of the true faith: 'In the name of the Lord God and Savior,' and so forth. In the same place, column five forty-nine. This too is a Pseudo-Isidorian forgery.
chunks.notes: Forged 'Roman council' under Julius I (337-352).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ejusdem Julii papae epistola ad Orientales pro S. Athanasio. Decuerat vos, fratres, alios ecclesiasticis instruere disciplinis, etc. Ibi col. 497. Est apocrypha.
chunks.english: The letter of the same Pope Julius to the Easterners on behalf of Saint Athanasius: 'It would have been fitting for you, brothers, to instruct others in ecclesiastical disciplines,' and so forth. In the same place, column four ninety-seven. It is apocryphal.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Responsio Orientalium pariter apocrypha. Licet circa omnes, etc. Ibi col. 502. Rescriptum Julii ad eosdem Orientales. Decuerat vos adversus sanctam Romanam et apostolicam Ecclesiam, etc. Ibi col. 506. Apocryphum.
chunks.english: The response of the Easterners, likewise apocryphal: 'Although concerning all,' and so forth. In the same place, column five oh two. The rescript of Julius to the same Easterners: 'It would have been fitting for you against the holy Roman and apostolic Church,' and so forth. In the same place, column five oh six. Apocryphal.
chunks.notes: Fabricated Athanasian controversy correspondence.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Athanasii et Aegyptiorum synodica ad Liberium papam apocrypha. Vestrae beatissimae paternitatis jura, etc. Eodem tom. II Concil., col. 817. Liberii rescriptum ad eosdem item supposititium. Olim et ab initio, etc. Ibi col. 819.
chunks.english: The synodical letter of Athanasius and the Egyptians to Pope Liberius, apocryphal: 'The rights of your most blessed fatherhood,' and so forth. In the same volume two of the Councils, column eight seventeen. The rescript of Liberius to them, likewise spurious: 'Long ago and from the beginning,' and so forth. In the same place, column eight nineteen.
chunks.notes: Forged under Pope Liberius (352-366).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Athanasii et Aegyptiorum ad Felicem papam epistola apocrypha. Vestro sancto suggerimus apostolatui, etc. Ibi col. 993. Felicis responsio. Sacram vestram synodicam, etc. Ibi col. 999. Alia ejusdem ad eosdem. Gratia vobis, etc. Ibi col. 1005. Utraque supposititia.
chunks.english: The letter of Athanasius and the Egyptians to Pope Felix, apocryphal: 'We suggest to your holy apostolate,' and so forth. In the same place, column nine ninety-three. The response of Felix: 'Your sacred synodical letter,' and so forth. In the same place, column nine ninety-nine. Another of the same to them: 'Grace to you,' and so forth. In the same place, column ten oh five. Both spurious.
chunks.notes: More forgeries under Pope Felix II (355-365, though considered an antipope).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Liberii epistola ad universos episcopos. Nihil est quod stare, etc. Ibi col. 815. Apocrypha.
chunks.english: The letter of Liberius to all bishops: 'There is nothing that can stand,' and so forth. In the same place, column eight fifteen. Apocryphal.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Damasus Paulino. Per filium meum Vitalem, etc. [Col. 0256D] Ibi col. 1014. Haec sincera Damasi epistola sumpta est ex collectione Hispanica.
chunks.english: Damasus to Paulinus: 'Through my son Vitalis,' and so forth. In the same place, column ten fourteen. This genuine letter of Damasus is taken from the Hispanic collection.
chunks.notes: First GENUINE papal letter in this section (Pope Damasus, 366-384).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postea Pseudo-Isidorus alteram genuinam ejusdem Damasi epistolam ad eumdem Paulinum ex Hispanica collectione sumptam, quae Confessio fidei ad eumdem Paulinum alias inscribitur, in tres partes dissecuit, iisdemque tres apocryphas epistolas interseruit, tabula sex capitulorum praemissa, cui eae partes tres genuinae epistolae, ac tres epistolae supposititiae post tabulam subjectae respondent hoc modo.
chunks.english: Afterward, Pseudo-Isidore took another genuine letter of the same Damasus to the same Paulinus from the Hispanic collection (which is elsewhere inscribed 'Confession of Faith to the same Paulinus') and cut it into three parts, and among these he interwove three apocryphal letters. A table of six chapters is placed first, to which correspond the three parts of the genuine letter and the three spurious letters placed after the table, in this manner:
chunks.notes: Sophisticated forgery technique: genuine letter chopped up and interlarded with forgeries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: I. Ejusdem Damasi ad Paulinum. Post Nicaenum concilium, aliud in urbe Romana postea congregatum est, etc., uti tom. II Concil., col. 1061, usque ad illa, Si quis de Patre et Filio bene senserit, de Spiritu sancto autem non recte habuerit, haereticus est.
chunks.english: First, of the same Damasus to Paulinus: 'After the Nicene council, another was later gathered in the city of Rome,' and so forth, as in volume two of the Councils, column ten sixty-one, up to these words: 'If anyone thinks rightly about the Father and the Son, but does not hold correctly concerning the Holy Spirit, he is a heretic.'
chunks.notes: Part 1 of genuine Damasus letter (beginning).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Est prima pars laudatae epistolae, seu confessionis fidei ad Paulinum; nisi quod omittitur hoc loco decretum adversus eos qui de Ecclesiis suis ad alias migrant: quod infra subjicitur tit. 6.
chunks.english: This is the first part of the aforementioned letter, or confession of faith, to Paulinus; except that there is omitted at this point the decree against those who migrate from their churches to others—which is added below under title six.
chunks.notes: Editorial surgery on genuine text.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: II. Epistola Stephani ac trium conciliorum Africae ad Damasum. Notum vestrae facimus beatitudini, etc. Ibi col. 1018. Est supposititia.
chunks.english: Second, the letter of Stephen and three councils of Africa to Damasus: 'We make known to your blessedness,' and so forth. In the same place, column ten eighteen. It is spurious.
chunks.notes: Forgery #1 inserted.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: III. Quod omnes haeretici de Filio Dei et Spiritu sancto male sentientes in perfidia Judaeorum et gentilium inveniuntur. Quod si quis patiatur Deum Patrem, et Deum Filium, et Spiritum sanctum deos dici, etc.
chunks.english: Third, that all heretics who think wrongly about the Son of God and the Holy Spirit are found in the perfidy of Jews and gentiles: 'But if anyone allows God the Father and God the Son and the Holy Spirit to be called gods,' and so forth.
chunks.notes: Part 2 of genuine Damasus letter (middle section).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Est postrema pars laudatae genuinae epistolae ad Paulinum. Ibi col. 1063. Sed post ultima verba sine dubio credamus, in cod. Isid. adduntur alia nonnulla quae in puris Hispanicae collectionis exemplaribus non leguntur.
chunks.english: This is the last part of the aforementioned genuine letter to Paulinus. In the same place, column ten sixty-three. But after the final words 'let us believe without doubt,' in the Isidorian manuscript are added certain other things which are not read in the pure exemplars of the Hispanic collection.
chunks.notes: Genuine text with Pseudo-Isidorian additions at end.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: IV. Epistola Damasi ad Prosperum Numidiae et alios Afros. Licet, fratres charissimi, nobis sint nota, etc. Ibi col. 1025. Apocrypha.
chunks.english: Fourth, the letter of Damasus to Prosper of Numidia and other Africans: 'Although, dearest brothers, these things are known to us,' and so forth. In the same place, column ten twenty-five. Apocryphal.
chunks.notes: Forgery #2 inserted.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: V. De sacerdotibus qui de Ecclesiis suis ad alias migrant. Eos autem sacerdotes qui de Ecclesiis ad Ecclesias, etc. Ibi col. 1062, usque ad verba quiescat in Domino.
chunks.english: Fifth, concerning priests who migrate from their churches to others: 'But those priests who go from church to church,' and so forth. In the same place, column ten sixty-two, up to the words 'may he rest in the Lord.'
chunks.notes: Part 3 of genuine Damasus letter (decree previously omitted, now inserted here).

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Est decretum memoratae genuinae epistolae Damasi ad Paulinum, quod antea omissum [Col. 0257B] fuerat.
chunks.english: This is the decree from the aforementioned genuine letter of Damasus to Paulinus, which had been omitted earlier.
chunks.notes: Completing the dismembered genuine letter.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Epistola synodica Damasi ad Illyrios episcopos. Credimus sanctam fidem vestram, etc., sumpta est ex versione Epiphanii in Historia Tripartita lib. V, c. 29, cui Pseudo-Isidorus addidit chronicam notam spuriam, Dat. XVII kal. Nov. Siricio et Ardabure VV. CC. consulibus. Vide tom. II Concil., col. 1031.
chunks.english: The synodical letter of Damasus to the bishops of Illyricum: 'We believe your holy faith,' and so forth. This is taken from the translation of Epiphanius in the Tripartite History, book five, chapter twenty-nine, to which Pseudo-Isidore added a spurious chronological notation: 'Given on the seventeenth day before the Kalends of November, in the consulship of Siricius and Ardaburius, most noble men.' See volume two of the Councils, column ten thirty-one.
chunks.notes: Genuine letter with forged dating clause added.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Damasi epistola apocrypha ad episcopos Italiae. Optaveram, dilectissimi, etc. Ibi col. 1033.
chunks.english: The apocryphal letter of Damasus to the bishops of Italy: 'I had hoped, most beloved,' and so forth. In the same place, column ten thirty-three.
chunks.notes: Another Damasus forgery.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Siricii epistolae tres, omnes sincerae ex collectione Hispanica, una ad Himerium, [CCXXXI] altera ad diversos contra Jovinianum, tertia ad orthodoxos per diversas provincias. Ibi. col. 1212, 1218 et 1223.
chunks.english: Three letters of Siricius, all genuine from the Hispanic collection: one to Himerius, another to various persons against Jovinian, the third to the orthodox throughout various provinces. In the same place, columns twelve twelve, twelve eighteen, and twelve twenty-three.
chunks.notes: Pope Siricius (384-399): genuine letters.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Anastasii duae supposititiae, prior ad Germaniae et Burgundiae episcopos, secunda ad Nerianum nobilissimum virum. Ibi col. 1428 et 1430.
chunks.english: Two spurious letters of Anastasius: the first to the bishops of Germany and Burgundy, the second to Nerianus, a most noble man. In the same place, columns fourteen twenty-eight and fourteen thirty.
chunks.notes: Pope Anastasius I (399-401): forgeries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Innocentii epistolae tres et viginti, omnes sincerae, ex Hispanica collectione excerptae, quae leguntur tom. III Conciliorum, ab epist. 1 ejusdem pontificis [Col. 0257C] usque ad 23, col. 13 et seqq.
chunks.english: Twenty-three letters of Innocent, all genuine, excerpted from the Hispanic collection, which are read in volume three of the Councils, from epistle one of the same pontiff to twenty-three, column thirteen and following.
chunks.notes: Pope Innocent I (401-417): extensive genuine correspondence preserved.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Carthaginensis concilii epistola ad Innocentium, et rescriptum Innocentii. Dein epistola concilii Milevitani ad eumdem pontificem cum hujus rescripto. Item epistola familiaris quinque episcoporum Africae ad ipsum Innocentium, ei hujus responsio. Tandem epistola familiaris Innocentii ad Aurelium.
chunks.english: The letter of the Council of Carthage to Innocent, and Innocent's rescript. Then the letter of the Council of Milevis to the same pontiff with his rescript. Likewise the familiar letter of five African bishops to Innocent himself, and his response. Finally, the familiar letter of Innocent to Aurelius.
chunks.notes: Genuine Pelagian controversy correspondence.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hae septem epistolae leguntur in collectione Gallicana hoc tomo impressa, ex qua Pseudo-Isidorus illas hausit. Vide infra a col. 128 ad 164.
chunks.english: These seven letters are read in the Gallic collection printed in this volume, from which Pseudo-Isidore drew them. See below from column one twenty-eight to one sixty-four.
chunks.notes: Source identification: another Gallic collection used by Pseudo-Isidore.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Zosimi epistolae duae ex collectione Hispanica decerptae, una ad Esychium, altera ad clerum Ravennensem. Exstant in epistolis Zosimi t. III Concil., ep. 1 et 2.
chunks.english: Two letters of Zosimus taken from the Hispanic collection: one to Hesychius, the other to the clergy of Ravenna. They are found among the letters of Zosimus in volume three of the Councils, epistles one and two.
chunks.notes: Pope Zosimus (417-418): genuine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Bonifacii papae epistola ad Honorium Augustum cum hujus responsione; et alia ejusdem pontificis ad episcopos Galliae. Sunt sincerae, ex Hispanica collectione sumptae. Exstant eodem tom. III Concil. inter Bonifacii epistolas.
chunks.english: The letter of Pope Boniface to the Emperor Honorius with his response, and another of the same pontiff to the bishops of Gaul. They are genuine, taken from the Hispanic collection. They are found in the same volume three of the Councils among the letters of Boniface.
chunks.notes: Pope Boniface I (418-422): genuine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Coelestini epistolae tres item ex Hispanica, una ad [Col. 0257D] Venerium et alios episcopos Gallos, altera ad episcopos per Viennensem et Narbonensem provinciam constitutos, tertia ad episcopos Apuliae. In Conciliis sunt epist. 1, 2 et 3 hujus pontificis.
chunks.english: Three letters of Celestine, likewise from the Hispanic collection: one to Venerius and other Gallic bishops, another to the bishops established throughout the province of Vienne and Narbonne, the third to the bishops of Apulia. In the Councils these are epistles one, two, and three of this pontiff.
chunks.notes: Pope Celestine I (422-432): genuine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Xysti III una apocrypha ad episcopos Orientales. Est tom. IV Concil., epist. 3.
chunks.english: One apocryphal letter of Sixtus the Third to the bishops of the East. It is in volume four of the Councils, epistle three.
chunks.notes: Pope Sixtus III (432-440): one forgery.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ex collectione Hispanica postea subduntur eodem plane ordine epistolae novem et triginta S. Leonis, et aliorum, quas in descriptione collectionis Hispanicae recensuimus cap. 4, n. 6.
chunks.english: From the Hispanic collection there are then added in exactly the same order thirty-nine letters of Saint Leo and others, which we enumerated in the description of the Hispanic collection in chapter four, number six.
chunks.notes: Pope Leo I (440-461): major corpus of genuine letters.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hilari papae tria documenta ex eadem collectione Hispanica, id est synodale decretum, et duae epistolae ad Ascanium Tarraconensem. Sunt in Conciliis epist. 1, 2 et 3.
chunks.english: Three documents of Pope Hilary from the same Hispanic collection: namely, a synodal decree and two letters to Ascanius of Tarragona. They are in the Councils, epistles one, two, and three.
chunks.notes: Pope Hilary (461-468): genuine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Simplicii papae epistola ad Zenonem Spalensem, et Acacii epistola ad Simplicium ex eadem collectione. Prior est in Conciliis epist. 1 ejusdem pontificis, altera est post epist. 8.
chunks.english: The letter of Pope Simplicius to Zeno of Spalato, and the letter of Acacius to Simplicius, from the same collection. The former is in the Councils as epistle one of the same pontiff; the latter is after epistle eight.
chunks.notes: Pope Simplicius (468-483): genuine correspondence.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Felicis II (apud Pseudo-Isidorum III) epistolae [Col. 0258A] tres ex eadem Hispanica, una ad Siculos, altera ad Acacium, tertia ad Zenonem Spalensem. In Conciliis epist. 7, 6 et 8.
chunks.english: Three letters of Felix the Second (Felix the Third in Pseudo-Isidore) from the same Hispanic collection: one to the Sicilians, another to Acacius, the third to Zeno of Spalato. In the Councils, epistles seven, six, and eight.
chunks.notes: Pope Felix III (483-492), mislabeled Felix II by Pseudo-Isidore: genuine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Decretum Gelasii papae de libris recipiendis vel non recipiendis, quod incipit: Post propheticas et evangelicas Scripturas, etc., uti tomo V, Concil. col. 386.
chunks.english: The decree of Pope Gelasius concerning books to be received or not received, which begins: 'After the prophetic and evangelical Scriptures,' and so forth, as in volume five of the Councils, column three eighty-six.
chunks.notes: Pope Gelasius I (492-496): the 'Gelasian Decree'—genuine or early forgery, debated.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hoc decretum sincerum exscriptum fuit ex aliqua vetustiore collectione seu codice Gallicano, cujusmodi sunt Colbertinus 784, de quo diximus part., II c. 6; et alius Colbert. 3368, de quo ibidem, cap. 8.
chunks.english: This genuine decree was copied from some older Gallic collection or manuscript, such as Colbert seven eighty-four, about which we spoke in part two, chapter six, and another Colbert manuscript thirty-three sixty-eight, about which we spoke in the same place, chapter eight.
chunks.notes: Identifying Pseudo-Isidore's Gallic sources for this decree.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aliud ejusdem decretum generale, quod alias inscribitur: Epistola ad episcopos Lucaniae et Siciliae. Item alia ejusdem epistola ad Sicilienses. Haec duo ex Hispanica collectione excerpta leguntur tom. V Concil. epist. 7 et 10.
chunks.english: Another general decree of the same pope, which is elsewhere inscribed 'Letter to the bishops of Lucania and Sicily.' Likewise another letter of the same to the Sicilians. These two, excerpted from the Hispanic collection, are read in volume five of the Councils, epistles seven and ten.
chunks.notes: More genuine Gelasius letters.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ejusdem Gelasii epistolae quatuor, prior Fausto magistro, secunda Anastasio imperatori, tertia Dardaniae episcopis, quarta ad Orientales. Hae sumptae ex Gallicana collectione hoc tomo impressa cap. 48, [Col. 0258B] 49, 50 et 51.
chunks.english: Four letters of the same Gelasius: the first to Faustus the master, the second to the Emperor Anastasius, the third to the bishops of Dardania, the fourth to the Easterners. These were taken from the Gallic collection printed in this volume, chapters forty-eight, forty-nine, fifty, and fifty-one.
chunks.notes: More Gelasius material from yet another Gallic collection.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Anastasii papae epistola ad Anastasium Augustum ex collectione Hispanica. Exstat tom. V Concil., epist. 1 inter illas ejusdem pontificis.
chunks.english: The letter of Pope Anastasius to the Emperor Anastasius, from the Hispanic collection. It is found in volume five of the Councils, epistle one among those of the same pontiff.
chunks.notes: Pope Anastasius II (496-498): genuine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Symmachi papae epistola ad Caesarium Arelatensem, item ex Hispanica. Ibi epist. 5 inter Symmachi epistolas.
chunks.english: The letter of Pope Symmachus to Caesarius of Arles, likewise from the Hispanic collection. In the same place, epistle five among the letters of Symmachus.
chunks.notes: Pope Symmachus (498-514): genuine letter.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Subjiciuntur tria genuina concilia sub eodem Symmacho ex Hadriana collectione, quae tom. V Concil. inscribuntur synodus I, III et IV.
chunks.english: There are added three genuine councils under the same Symmachus from the Hadrianic collection, which in volume five of the Councils are inscribed as synod one, three, and four.
chunks.notes: Roman synods under Symmachus: genuine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In plerisque codicibus Isidorianis inseruntur hoc loco duae aliae synodi sub eodem pontifice, quas fictitias probavimus, nec non alia sincera sumpta ex Ennodio. Verum haec in ms. Vat. 630, in finem collectionis rejiciuntur, de quibus inferius redibit sermo.
chunks.english: In most Isidorian manuscripts there are inserted at this point two other synods under the same pontiff, which we have proved to be fictitious, as well as another genuine one taken from Ennodius. But in Vatican manuscript six thirty these are relegated to the end of the collection, about which we shall speak further below.
chunks.notes: Variant placement of Symmachian forgeries in different MSS; VAT 630 has them as appendix.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hormisdae epistolae octo, et duae ad Hormisdam eodem ordine quo eas designavimus in collectione Hispanica, ex qua Pseudo-Isidorus eas derivavit.
chunks.english: Eight letters of Hormisdas, and two to Hormisdas, in the same order as we designated them in the Hispanic collection, from which Pseudo-Isidore derived them.
chunks.notes: Pope Hormisdas (514-523): genuine letters.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Joannis I papae epistolae duae, una ad Zachariam [Col. 0258C] archiepiscopum, alia ad episcopos Italos. Sunt supposititiae.
chunks.english: Two letters of Pope John the First: one to Archbishop Zachary, the other to the bishops of Italy. They are spurious.
chunks.notes: Pope John I (523-526): forgeries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Felicis III (apud Pseudo-Isidorum IV) aliae duae epistolae item supposititiae, prima ad omnes episcopos, altera ad Sabinam.
chunks.english: Two other letters of Felix the Third (Felix the Fourth in Pseudo-Isidore), likewise spurious: the first to all bishops, the second to Sabina.
chunks.notes: Pope Felix IV (526-530): forgeries. Numbering confusion continues.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Bonifacii II epistola ad Eulalium apocrypha. Joannis II apocrypha epistola ad Valerium episcopum. Agapeti I epistola ad Antemium episcopum CP. item apocrypha.
chunks.english: The letter of Boniface the Second to Eulalius, apocryphal. The apocryphal letter of John the Second to Bishop Valerius. The letter of Agapetus the First to Bishop Anthemius of Constantinople, likewise apocryphal.
chunks.notes: Popes Boniface II (530-532), John II (533-535), Agapetus I (535-536): all forgeries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Amatoris episcopi epistola ad Silverium papam, et hujus responsio: ambae supposititiae.
chunks.english: The letter of Bishop Amator to Pope Silverius, and his response: both spurious.
chunks.notes: Pope Silverius (536-537): forged correspondence.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vigilii epistola sincera ad Profuturum, sumpta ex collectione Hispanica; sed apud Pseudo-Isidorum inscribitur ad Euterium ob mendosam lectionem, de qua videsis cap. 4, n. 15.
chunks.english: The genuine letter of Vigilius to Profuturus, taken from the Hispanic collection; but in Pseudo-Isidore it is inscribed to Euterius on account of a corrupt reading, concerning which see chapter four, number fifteen.
chunks.notes: Pope Vigilius (537-555): genuine letter with corrupted addressee name.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Huic porro epistolae ille ex suo duo capita addidit sextum et septimum.
chunks.english: To this letter he added from his own work two chapters, the sixth and seventh.
chunks.notes: Genuine letter interpolated with forged additions.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Pelagii I epistola ad Vigilium episcopum, in Vat. 631 ad Julium, supposititia. Joannis III papae decreta ad Germanos et Gallos, [Col. 0258D] item supposititia.
chunks.english: The letter of Pelagius the First to Bishop Vigilius (in Vatican six thirty-one, to Julius), spurious. The decrees of Pope John the Third to the Germans and Gauls, likewise spurious.
chunks.notes: Popes Pelagius I (556-561) and John III (561-574): forgeries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Benedicti papae apocrypha epistola ad David episcopum. Pelagii II epistolae tres, una ad episcopos synodi CP., secunda ad Benignum archiepiscopum, tertia ad universos episcopos praesertim Campaniae et Italiae, omnes apocryphae.
chunks.english: The apocryphal letter of Pope Benedict to Bishop David. Three letters of Pelagius the Second: one to the bishops of the synod of Constantinople, the second to Archbishop Benignus, the third to all bishops especially of Campania and Italy—all apocryphal.
chunks.notes: Popes Benedict I (575-579) and Pelagius II (579-590): forgeries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: S. Gregorii Magni epistolae sex. Tres priores genuinae ex collectione Hispanica sumptae, prima et secunda ad Leandrum episcopum Hispalensem, tertia vero ad Recaredum regem, quae leguntur lib. I Registri, epist. 43 editionis Maurinae, et lib. IX, epist. 121 et 122.
chunks.english: Six letters of Saint Gregory the Great. The first three are genuine, taken from the Hispanic collection: the first and second to Leander, bishop of Seville, and the third to King Recared. These are read in book one of the Register, epistle forty-three of the Maurist edition, and in book nine, epistles one twenty-one and one twenty-two.
chunks.notes: Pope Gregory I (590-604): mixture of genuine and interpolated letters.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postea subjiciuntur tres aliae ejusdem Gregorii epistolae, quae vel ex Registro ipsius, vel ex aliis collectionibus Gallicanis sumi potuerunt: una ad Secundinum, quae exstat lib. IX, epist. 52, sed apud Pseudo-Isidorum longo additamento interpolata est, quod tamen ab alio antiquiori impostore confictum [Col. 0259A] videbimus part. IV, c. 8;
chunks.english: Afterward are added three other letters of the same Gregory, which could have been taken either from his Register or from other Gallic collections. One to Secundinus, which is found in book nine, epistle fifty-two, but in Pseudo-Isidore it is interpolated with a long addition, which however we shall see was fabricated by another, earlier impostor in part four, chapter eight.
chunks.notes: Gregory's letter to Secundinus: genuine but with earlier interpolation adopted by Pseudo-Isidore.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: altera ad Teoctistam patriciam, quae habetur lib. XI, epist. 45; tertia ad Augustinum, quae exhibet hujus interrogationes et Gregorii responsa, uti leguntur eodem lib. XI, epist. 64.
chunks.english: Another to the patrician Theoctista, which is found in book eleven, epistle forty-five; the third to Augustine, which presents his questions and Gregory's responses, as they are read in the same book eleven, epistle sixty-four.
chunks.notes: Two more genuine Gregory letters: to Theoctista and the famous 'Libellus Responsionum' to Augustine of Canterbury.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Synodus sub eodem Gregorio Magno cum sex canonibus usque ad verba subire appetit servitutem, uti habetur tom. VI Concil., col. 1310.
chunks.english: A synod under the same Gregory the Great with six canons, up to the words 'desires to undergo servitude,' as it is found in volume six of the Councils, column thirteen ten.
chunks.notes: Roman synod under Gregory: genuine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Decreta papae Gregorii Junioris. Est synodus Romana impressa tom. VIII Concil., col. 185. In hac synodo primaeva Pseudo-Isidori collectio desinit.
chunks.english: The decrees of Pope Gregory the Younger. This is the Roman synod printed in volume eight of the Councils, column one eighty-five. With this synod the original Pseudo-Isidorian collection ends.
chunks.notes: Gregory II (715-731): Roman synod of 721. END OF ORIGINAL PSEUDO-ISIDORE.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Porro in ms. Vat. 630, ex quo hujus collectionis descriptionem expressimus, folium album sequitur, quod confirmat eamdem collectionem in laudata synodo desiisse, adeo ut quae post idem folium in eodem codice subjiciuntur, posteriori additamento accensenda sint.
chunks.english: Furthermore, in Vatican manuscript six thirty, from which we have drawn this description of the collection, a blank page follows, which confirms that the same collection ended with the aforementioned synod, so that what is added after that same page in the same manuscript must be reckoned as a later addition.
chunks.notes: Physical evidence in VAT 630: blank page marking end of original collection.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Appendices to Pseudo-Isidore in the Earliest Manuscript
chunks.latin: Subduntur autem eodem charactere totius codicis sequentia documenta, quae ad Symmachi papae causam pertinent.
chunks.english: But there are added in the same handwriting as the entire manuscript the following documents, which pertain to the case of Pope Symmachus.
chunks.notes: First appendix: Symmachian forgeries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Libellus apologeticus Ennodii pro synodo palmari [Col. 0259B] Romana, et pro Symmacho. Vide tom. V Concil, col. 479.
chunks.english: The apologetic pamphlet of Ennodius for the Roman palm synod and for Symmachus. See volume five of the Councils, column four seventy-nine.
chunks.notes: Genuine work of Ennodius defending Symmachus.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Synodus V et VI, sub eodem Symmacho, quas n. 7 supposititias probavimus. Exstant ibidem, col. 501 et 509.
chunks.english: Synods five and six under the same Symmachus, which we proved spurious in number seven above. They are found in the same place, columns five oh one and five oh nine.
chunks.notes: Two forged Roman synods under Symmachus.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ennodii epistolae duae Symmacho attributae, una ad Laurentium Mediolanensem, quae incipit: Prodit religiosae motum conscientiae. In Ennodii operibus legitur, sed cum titulo: Dictio tertia Ennodii data Stephano V. S. vicario dicenda Maximo episcopo:
chunks.english: Two letters of Ennodius attributed to Symmachus: one to Laurentius of Milan, which begins 'The movement of a religious conscience comes forth.' It is read in the works of Ennodius, but with the title: 'Third speech of Ennodius given to Stephen, vicar of the Apostolic See, to be spoken to Bishop Maximus.'
chunks.notes: Genuine Ennodius work falsely attributed to Symmachus.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: altera ad Liberium patricium, quae apud eumdem Ennodium est epist. 1 libri V.
chunks.english: The other to the patrician Liberius, which in the same Ennodius is epistle one of book five.
chunks.notes: Another genuine Ennodius letter misattributed to Symmachus.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum porro haec documenta, praesertim synodi duae supposititiae, Pseudo-Isidoro tribuantur, et in aliis collectionis Isidorianae exemplaribus post tres sinceras Symmachi synodos inveniantur inserta; haec posterioribus Pseudo-Isidori curis accessisse videntur; et idcirco descripta in fine laudati codicis Vat. 630, qui in antecedentibus foliis primigeniam collectionem [Col. 0259C] his documentis expertem continet.
chunks.english: Moreover, since these documents, especially the two spurious synods, are attributed to Pseudo-Isidore and are found inserted in other exemplars of the Isidorian collection after the three genuine synods of Symmachus, these seem to have been added by Pseudo-Isidore in later revisions. And therefore they are written at the end of the aforementioned manuscript Vatican six thirty, which in the preceding folios contains the original collection devoid of these documents.
chunks.notes: Explaining why Symmachian forgeries are appended: second edition of Pseudo-Isidore.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Post hujusmodi additamenta in eodem codice iterum pagina alba et vacua relinquitur; ac dein sequentia eodem charactere adjiciuntur.
chunks.english: After such additions, in the same manuscript another blank and empty page is left; and then the following items are added in the same handwriting.
chunks.notes: Another blank page marking another appendix.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Capitula Hadriano papae ascripta, et Angilramno episcopo Mediomatriciae tradita. Sunt capita 52, quorum ultimo alia capita 19 immediate annectuntur.
chunks.english: The chapters ascribed to Pope Hadrian and handed over to Angilramnus, bishop of Metz. There are fifty-two chapters, to the last of which are immediately attached another nineteen chapters.
chunks.notes: The 'Capitula Angilramni'—another famous Pseudo-Isidorian forgery.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec, quae edita sunt tom. VIII Concil., col. 599, supposititia et ab impostore post primum fetum digesta, demonstravimus n. 8.
chunks.english: These, which were published in volume eight of the Councils, column five ninety-nine, we demonstrated in number eight above to be spurious and composed by the impostor after his first production.
chunks.notes: Second-generation Pseudo-Isidorian forgery, added later.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Disputatio Constantii imperatoris et Liberii Romani pontificis. Constantius imperator dixit, etc., ut in Hist. Tripartita, lib. V, c. 17.
chunks.english: The disputation between the Emperor Constantius and Liberius, Roman pontiff: 'The Emperor Constantius said,' and so forth, as in the Tripartite History, book five, chapter seventeen.
chunks.notes: Genuine historical text from Cassiodorus/Epiphanius.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnullae sanctiones sparsim collectae ex actionibus concilii Chalcedonensis, quas in aliis quoque collectionibus invenimus.
chunks.english: Certain decrees collected here and there from the acts of the Council of Chalcedon, which we also find in other collections.
chunks.notes: Genuine Chalcedonian material.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: De mutatione episcoporum ex lib. XII Hist. Tripartitae,
chunks.english: Concerning the transfer of bishops, from book twelve of the Tripartite History.
chunks.notes: Genuine historical excerpt; text appears to end mid-sentence in the Latin source.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: cap. 8 et 11. Item ex epistola Pelagii papae. Quod non debeat episcopus de civitate ad civitatem transire.
chunks.english: chapters eight and eleven. Another selection from the letter of Pope Pelagius: That a bishop ought not to transfer from one city to another.
chunks.notes: Continuing citation references from previous section

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hic est codicis finis. Capitula Hadriano I afficta, in aliis Isidorianis codicibus qui pseudosynodos Symmachi suo loco inserunt, subduntur post Gregorii Junioris decreta, et ad Isidori collectionem (in qua tantum inventa sunt, nec non in iis quae ex Isidoriana proveniunt) pertinere creduntur.
chunks.english: This is the end of the manuscript. The chapters falsely attributed to Hadrian the First, which appear in other Isidorian manuscripts that insert the pseudo-synods of Symmachus in their proper place, are placed after the decrees of Gregory the Younger and are believed to belong to Isidore's collection, since they are found only there and in works derived from the Isidorian tradition.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Certe ipsorum capitulorum suppositio Pseudo-Isidori indolem refert, et ex supposititiis quidem epistolis ab eo conditis eadem fuisse excerpta observavimus, adeo ut idem omnium artifex perspiciatur.
chunks.english: Certainly the forgery of these chapters displays the characteristic methods of Pseudo-Isidore, and we have observed that they were excerpted from the same forged letters composed by him, so that the same artificer of all these works becomes apparent.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum vero Pseudo-Isidorus in praefatione scripserit se subjecturum decreta praesulum Romanorum, non ad Hadrianum usque, sed usque ad S. Gregorium; ea capitula ad primigenium ejus fetum non pertinuisse dicenda sunt, sed postea ab eodem fuisse conficta; ac propterea jure in nostro codice separatim inter additamenta referuntur.
chunks.english: However, since Pseudo-Isidore wrote in his preface that he would append the decrees of Roman bishops not all the way to Hadrian but only to Saint Gregory, we must conclude that these chapters did not belong to his original production but were fabricated by the same author later on. For this reason they are rightly listed separately among the additions in our manuscript.
chunks.notes: Gregory refers to Gregory I (the Great)

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Codex Vat. 631, qui quondam fuit cardinalis Petri episcopi Rothomagensis, saeculo XII scriptus, omnia continet quae sunt in Vat. 630, exceptis duabus postremis additionibus de mutatione episcoporum, et ex epistola Pelagii papae.
chunks.english: Vatican manuscript six hundred thirty-one, which once belonged to Cardinal Peter, Bishop of Rouen, written in the twelfth century, contains everything that is in Vatican six hundred thirty, with the exception of the two final additions concerning the transfer of bishops and the selection from Pope Pelagius's letter.
chunks.notes: Rothomagensis = Rouen

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Solum ordo alicubi mutatur: et cum ea quae ad Symmachum pertinent additamenta suo loco descripta sunt post tres sinceras ejusdem pontificis synodos; tum vero Hadriani capitula cum reliquis post Gregorii Junioris decreta sine ullo additamenti indicio subjiciuntur.
chunks.english: Only the ordering is changed in some places: the additions pertaining to Symmachus are described in their proper place after the three genuine synods of that pontiff, while the chapters of Hadrian are then appended with the rest after the decrees of Gregory the Younger without any indication that they are additions.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Illud vero hujus codicis peculiare est, quod post Isidorianam collectionem addit totam collectionem Hadrianam, et versionem concilii VI sub Agathone pontifice.
chunks.english: What is unique to this manuscript, however, is that after the Isidorian collection it adds the entire Hadrianic collection and the version of the Sixth Council under Pope Agatho.
chunks.notes: Council VI = Third Council of Constantinople (680-681)

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Manuscripts with Incomplete or Modified Isidorian Collections
chunks.latin: § 6. De quibusdam mss. codicibus qui Isidorianam collectionem non omnino puram aut integram continent. Quaedam epistolae apocryphae Pseudo-Isidoro perperam attributae.
chunks.english: Section Six: Concerning certain manuscripts that contain the Isidorian collection in a form that is not entirely pure or complete. Certain apocryphal letters wrongly attributed to Pseudo-Isidore.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quaenam sit pura et integra Isidoriana collectio hactenus explicavimus. Nunc de quibusdam codicibus agendum qui eam cum additamentis ab ipsa alienis, vel etiam non omnino integram praeferunt.
chunks.english: We have explained thus far what constitutes the pure and complete Isidorian collection. Now we must discuss certain manuscripts that present it with additions foreign to the collection itself, or in a form that is not entirely complete.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Primo occurrit codex Vat. Ottobonianus 93 membranaceus in-folio, qui antiquissimo charactere minutiori scriptus, et in duas columnas distinctus, noni vel decimi saeculi aetatem exhibet.
chunks.english: First we encounter Vatican Ottoboni manuscript ninety-three, a parchment folio written in very ancient small characters and divided into two columns, dating from the ninth or tenth century.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Gallicanum olim fuisse manifestat pergamena ad muniendum codicem in fine annexa, quae actum continet Gallicani episcopi Parisiensis, et Thomae de Corcellis sacrae paginae professoris, et Ecclesiae Parisiensis decani sub Pio II pontifice.
chunks.english: That it was once French is made clear by the parchment attached at the end to protect the manuscript, which contains a document of the French Bishop of Paris and Thomas of Courcelles, professor of sacred scripture and Dean of the Church of Paris, under Pope Pius the Second.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem codex initio epistolas priscorum pontificum, dein canones conciliorum, postea subsequentium sedis apostolicae antistitum decreta, id est ipsam Isidorianam collectionem repraesentat.
chunks.english: This manuscript presents at the beginning the letters of the early pontiffs, then the canons of councils, and afterward the decrees of subsequent prelates of the apostolic see—that is, the Isidorian collection itself.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed cum initio et fine careat, eamdem collectionem non omnino integram praestat. Hunc vero defectum supplent duo alii codices, Casanatensis PP. Praedicatorum Minervae signatus A 11, 14, et Vat. 3791;
chunks.english: But since it lacks both beginning and end, it does not provide the collection in entirely complete form. This deficiency is supplied by two other manuscripts: Casanatensis of the Preaching Fathers of the Minerva, marked A eleven, fourteen, and Vatican thirty-seven ninety-one.
chunks.notes: PP. Praedicatorum = Dominicans; Minerva = church of Santa Maria sopra Minerva in Rome

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: ille enim omnino integer priscorum pontificum epistolas et canones conciliorum complectitur; iste vero subsequentium decreta pontificum ad finem usque; et ita hi codices cum laudato ms. Vat. Ottoboniano in sua cujusque parte conveniunt, ut is in duas veluti partes dissectus, duobus illis codicibus comprehendatur, ac quid in ipso sive initio, sive in fine desit, ex iisdem suppleri tute possit.
chunks.english: The former contains completely intact the letters of the early pontiffs and the canons of councils, while the latter has the decrees of subsequent pontiffs all the way to the end. Thus these manuscripts agree with the aforementioned Vatican Ottoboni manuscript in their respective portions, so that what is missing from it, whether at beginning or end, can safely be supplied from them, as if the Ottoboni manuscript had been divided into two parts that are contained in those two manuscripts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: P. Coustantius similes Isidorianos codices vidit, qui solum canonibus conciliorum carebant. Monet enim duplicem indicem capitulorum in iisdem fuisse praemissum, unum initio ante epistolas priscorum pontificum, qui et conciliorum canones quantumvis in iis codicibus omissos designat, alium ante epistolas posteriorum pontificum.
chunks.english: Father Coustant saw similar Isidorian manuscripts that lacked only the canons of councils. He notes that a double index of chapters was prefixed to them: one at the beginning before the letters of the early pontiffs, which also lists the canons of councils even though they were omitted from those manuscripts, and another before the letters of the later pontiffs.
chunks.notes: Pierre Coustant, French Maurist scholar (1654-1721)

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hos autem indices in memoratis tantummodo exemplis nacti sumus: primum quidem in Casanatensi (nam Vat. Ottob. initio mutilus est), secundum vero in Vat. Ottob. et in alio Vat. 3791.
chunks.english: We have found these indexes only in the aforementioned copies: the first indeed in the Casanatensis manuscript (since Vatican Ottoboni is mutilated at the beginning), and the second in Vatican Ottoboni and in the other Vatican thirty-seven ninety-one.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praeterea ille in suis codicibus quasdam pseudoepistolas reperit Damaso, Hieronymo et Leoni affictas, quas solum laudatis nostris codicibus simul insertas reperire licuit: ac proinde illos P. Coustantii nostris plane similes multo certius agnovimus.
chunks.english: Moreover, in his manuscripts he found certain pseudo-letters falsely attributed to Damasus, Jerome, and Leo, which we have been able to find inserted together only in our aforementioned manuscripts. Therefore we have recognized with much greater certainty that Father Coustant's manuscripts are plainly similar to ours.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Is porro easdem pseudoepistolas aeque ac caeteras supposititias Pseudo-Isidoro attribuit, eo quod eos solos Isidorianos codices nactus, puram Isidorianam collectionem in ipsis contineri credidit.
chunks.english: Furthermore, he attributed these same pseudo-letters, just like the other forgeries, to Pseudo-Isidore, because having found only these Isidorian manuscripts, he believed they contained the pure Isidorian collection.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nos vero, quibus contigit manuscriptum Vat. 630, omnium antiquissimum et integrum, aliosque similes puram Isidorianam collectionem praeseferentes inspicere; horum comparatione memorata exemplaria eamdem collectionem non omnino puram continere perspeximus.
chunks.english: We, however, who have had the opportunity to examine manuscript Vatican six hundred thirty, the most ancient and complete of all, and other similar manuscripts presenting the pure Isidorian collection, have recognized through comparison with these that the aforementioned copies do not contain the collection in entirely pure form.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quantum ad primam partem, quae priscorum pontificum epistolas et canones conciliorum comprehendit, illi quidem codices cum eadem ejusdem partis documenta eodem ordine praeferant quem in Vat. 630 notavimus, puri Isidoriani dici queunt.
chunks.english: As regards the first part, which comprises the letters of the early pontiffs and the canons of councils, those manuscripts can indeed be called pure Isidorian, since they present the documents of this same part in the same order that we noted in Vatican six hundred thirty.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Solum haec pars, ut ex Casanatensi integro didicimus, incipit a praefatione Isidori, omissis annotationibus atque notitiis quae in Vat. 630 praefationi praeeunt, in codicibus autem de quibus agimus, post finem secundae partis rejiciuntur, uti deteximus ex ms. Vat. 3791.
chunks.english: Only this part, as we learned from the complete Casanatensis manuscript, begins with the preface of Isidore, omitting the annotations and notices that precede the preface in Vatican six hundred thirty, which in the manuscripts we are discussing are relegated to the end after the conclusion of the second part, as we discovered from manuscript Vatican thirty-seven ninety-one.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postea sequitur index capitum 77, qui etsi deest in puris Isidori exemplis, documentis tamen Isidorianis plane respondet. Priores numeri 30 assignantur totidem pontificibus a S. Clemente usque ad Melchiadem, quorum epistolae in eadem parte describuntur: caeteri numeri 47 sunt illi qui ad concilia pertinent in totidem numeros distributa, ut alibi vidimus.
chunks.english: Afterward follows an index of seventy-seven chapters which, although absent from the pure Isidorian copies, nonetheless corresponds exactly to the Isidorian documents. The first thirty numbers are assigned to as many pontiffs from Saint Clement up to Miltiades, whose letters are copied in this same part. The remaining forty-seven numbers are those pertaining to councils divided into that many sections, as we have seen elsewhere.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In altera vero parte, quae posteriorum pontificum epistolas respicit, non leve est istorum codicum a puris Isidorianis discrimen.
chunks.english: In the second part, however, which concerns the letters of the later pontiffs, the divergence of these manuscripts from the pure Isidorian ones is not slight.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Indicem capitulorum huic parti praemissum, qui in iisdem tantum mss. inventus est, Isidoro quidem ascribi non posse duo suadent: 1o Quia pauciores epistolas recenset quam in Isidoriana collectione contineantur.
chunks.english: Two considerations argue that the index of chapters prefixed to this part, which is found only in these manuscripts, cannot be attributed to Isidore. First, because it lists fewer letters than are contained in the Isidorian collection.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: E septem, ex. gr., Gelasii epistolis duas tantum, et e quatuor documentis Symmachi unum solummodo indicat. Praeterea tabula manuscripti Casanatensis desinit in Anastasio, illa vero codicis Vat. Ottoboniani concluditur in Hormisda; cum Isidoriana collectio ipso auctore testante ad Gregorium usque perveniat.
chunks.english: For example, of Gelasius's seven letters it indicates only two, and of Symmachus's four documents only one. Moreover, the table in the Casanatensis manuscript ends with Anastasius, while that in Vatican Ottoboni manuscript concludes with Hormisdas, whereas the Isidorian collection, as the author himself testifies, extends all the way to Gregory.
chunks.notes: Gregory I (the Great)

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: 2o Quia idem epistolarum ordo qui in eo indice visitur, nec cum ordine aliorum codicum Isidori concordat, nec cum illo ipsius codicis Vat. Ottoboniani, aut alterius Vat. 3791.
chunks.english: Second, because the ordering of letters seen in that index agrees neither with the order in other Isidorian manuscripts nor with that of Vatican Ottoboni manuscript itself or of the other Vatican thirty-seven ninety-one.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In ipso autem corpore horum codicum epistolae non solum non respondent praemisso indici, verum etiam a collectione Isidoriana dissentiunt, et ex hoc praesertim discrimine hanc esse veram Isidori collectionem opinari non possumus.
chunks.english: In the actual body of these manuscripts, moreover, the letters not only fail to correspond to the prefixed index but also diverge from the Isidorian collection, and from this divergence especially we cannot consider this to be the genuine collection of Isidore.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In primis ordo epistolarum, ex. gr. S. Leonis Isidorianus non est. Pseudo-Isidorus enim Leonis epistolas 39 ex Hispanica collectione in suam transtulit eo plane ordine quo in Hispanica describuntur, uti probant codices Vat. 630, 631 et alii.
chunks.english: First of all, the ordering of the letters—for example, those of Saint Leo—is not Isidorian. For Pseudo-Isidore transferred thirty-nine letters of Leo from the Hispana collection into his own in exactly the same order in which they are copied in the Hispana, as Vatican manuscripts six hundred thirty and six hundred thirty-one and others prove.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc eumdem pariter ordinem retinuit auctor ejus auctioris collectionis Isidorianae, quam describemus capite sequenti. Licet enim is plura additamenta inseruerit, epistolas tamen ex Isidoro exscriptas eodem ordine retulit quem in Isidorianis codicibus nactus est.
chunks.english: Hence the author of that augmented Isidorian collection, which we shall describe in the following chapter, likewise retained this same order. For although he inserted many additions, he nevertheless preserved the letters copied from Isidore in the same order he found in the Isidorian manuscripts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: At in mss. de quibus in praesentiarum est sermo, alius omnino Leonis epistolarum ordo inducitur, eademque ordinis perturbatio est etiam in litteris Innocentii et aliorum.
chunks.english: But in the manuscripts of which we are currently speaking, an altogether different ordering of Leo's letters is introduced, and the same confusion of order exists also in the letters of Innocent and others.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Deinde in Damaso num. 15 et 22 bis affertur confessio fidei quam ille ad Paulinum misit, cujus initium est: Post Nicaenum concilium. Semel tantum in puris Isidorianis exemplis legitur.
chunks.english: Then under Damasus at numbers fifteen and twenty-two, the confession of faith he sent to Paulinus is cited twice, which begins with the words 'After the Council of Nicaea.' It is read only once in the pure Isidorian copies.
chunks.notes: Council of Nicaea = 325 AD

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praeterea in Leone epistola ad Africanos ea forma auctiori exhibetur, qua editioni Hadrianae quaedam capitula subnectuntur in ea editione omissa, cum Isidorus ex Hispanica aliam formam decurtatam dederit. Vide nostram admonitionem in ipsam epist. 12.
chunks.english: Moreover, in Leo's letter to the Africans it is presented in an augmented form in which certain chapters are appended that were omitted from the Hadrianic edition, whereas Isidore gave a different, abridged form from the Hispana. See our note on that letter number twelve.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Adde tandem quod adjiciuntur aliquot epistolae partim sincerae, partim spuriae, quae in puris Isidorianis mss. desunt.
chunks.english: Add finally that several letters are adjoined, partly genuine and partly spurious, which are lacking in the pure Isidorian manuscripts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ita in epistolis Damasi interseruntur tres (T. II Concil. col. 1017), quarum duae sunt apocryphae, una ejusdem ad Hieronymum, quae incipit: Cum multa corpora librorum; altera rescriptum Hieronymi incipiens: Supplex legi epistolas apostolatus vestri;
chunks.english: Thus among the letters of Damasus three are inserted (in volume two of the Councils, column one thousand seventeen), of which two are apocryphal: one from Damasus to Jerome beginning 'Since there are many bodies of books,' and another, Jerome's response beginning 'As a supplicant I read the letters of your apostleship.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: tertia autem Damasi sine titulo, cujus initium: Cum apostolicae sedi reverentiam debitam: quae est genuina, ex Graeco Latine reddita, ab Epiphano Scholastico Historiae Tripartitae inserta lib. IX, c. 15, ubi ad Orientales missa traditur.
chunks.english: The third, however, is from Damasus without title, beginning 'Since reverence is owed to the apostolic see.' This one is genuine, rendered into Latin from Greek, inserted by Epiphanius Scholasticus into the Tripartite History, book nine, chapter fifteen, where it is reported as having been sent to the Easterners.
chunks.notes: Tripartite History = ecclesiastical history compiled from Greek sources

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Leonis epistolis addititiae sunt decem et octo; et in his epistola 28 ad Flavianum ex diversis fontibus bis describitur, nec deest epistola apocrypha a nobis in appendicem tomi primi rejecta de privilegio chorepiscoporum seu presbyterorum ad universos Germaniae et Galliae Ecclesiarum episcopos,
chunks.english: Among the letters of Leo, eighteen are additions, and among these, letter twenty-eight to Flavian is copied twice from different sources. Nor is there lacking the apocryphal letter that we relegated to the appendix of the first volume concerning the privilege of chorepiscopi or presbyters to all the bishops of the churches of Germany and Gaul—
