chunks.notes: Chorepiscopi = rural bishops with limited authority

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: quam tamen anterius confictam discimus ex litteris Joannis III ab ipso Pseudo-Isidoro suppositis, in quibus eadem Leonis epistola allegatur.
chunks.english: which we learn was forged earlier, from the letters of John the Third fabricated by Pseudo-Isidore himself, in which this same letter of Leo is cited.
chunks.notes: Shows Pseudo-Isidore citing his own earlier forgeries

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praeterea post S. Gregorii litteras adduntur Epistolarum verba de diversis locis excerpta, nimirum ex variis S. Gregorii Magni epistolis:
chunks.english: Moreover, after Saint Gregory's letters are added 'Sayings from the Letters' excerpted from various places, namely from various letters of Saint Gregory the Great.
chunks.notes: Gregory I, pope 590-604

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: et post capitula Hadriani tria appenduntur: 1o Epistola papae Spalensis episcopi (id est S. Isidori Hispalensis) ad Laudefridum Cordubensem, quam ex ms. Hispanico edidit Loaisa, ac ex Loaisa refertur tom. VII Concil., col. 434.
chunks.english: and after the chapters of Hadrian three items are appended. First, a letter of the pope, the bishop of Spalensis—that is, Saint Isidore of Seville—to Laudefridus of Cordoba, which Loaisa published from a Spanish manuscript and which is reproduced from Loaisa in volume seven of the Councils, column four hundred thirty-four.
chunks.notes: Spalensis is an error for Hispalensis (Seville)

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: 2o Alia Isidori ad Masonem episcopum de restauratione sacerdotum, quae supposititia creditur a Joanne Morino lib. IV de Poenitentia, c. 15, n. 6.
chunks.english: Second, another letter of Isidore to Bishop Maso concerning the restoration of priests, which is believed to be spurious by Jean Morin in his work 'On Penance,' book four, chapter fifteen, number six.
chunks.notes: Jean Morin (1591-1659), French theologian

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: 3o Catalogus de nominibus haereticorum qui unitatem Ecclesiae turbaverunt. Incipit: Quidam etiam haeretici, etc., uti apud Gratianum caus. 24, q. 3, c. 39. Sumptus est ex lib. VIII Etymologiarum S. Isidori Hispalensis, c. 5.
chunks.english: Third, a catalog of the names of heretics who disturbed the unity of the Church. It begins 'Certain heretics also,' and so forth, as in Gratian's Decretum, cause twenty-four, question three, chapter thirty-nine. It is taken from book eight of the Etymologies of Saint Isidore of Seville, chapter five.
chunks.notes: Gratian's Decretum = 12th century compilation of canon law

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec postrema additamenta, quae ob codicem in fine mutilum absunt a Vat. Ottob. 93, nobis innotuerunt ex ms. Vat. 3791, in quo haec secunda pars integra conservatur.
chunks.english: These last additions, which are absent from Vatican Ottoboni ninety-three because that manuscript is mutilated at the end, became known to us from manuscript Vatican thirty-seven ninety-one, in which this second part is preserved intact.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hoc tam multiplex ejusmodi codicum discrimen a caeteris antiquioribus et purioribus Isidorianis exemplis nonne puram Isidori collectionem in illis non reperiri demonstrat?
chunks.english: Does not this manifold divergence of such manuscripts from the other older and purer Isidorian copies demonstrate that the pure collection of Isidore is not found in them?
chunks.notes: Rhetorical question affirming their impurity

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Neque vero quispiam suspicetur documenta addititia, praesertim illa tria apocrypha quae memoravimus, posterioribus Pseudo-Isidori curis accessisse.
chunks.english: Nor should anyone suspect that the additional documents, especially those three apocryphal ones we mentioned, were added through later efforts of Pseudo-Isidore.
chunks.notes: Referring to the forged Damasus-Jerome correspondence and Leo letter

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Id enim satis revincit praecipue codex Vat. 630, in quo cum posteriora ejusdem Pseudo-Isidori additamenta duplici appendice in fine descripta inveniantur, dubitare non licet quin ea quoque addititia laudatorum codicum documenta ibidem cum caeteris subnexa fuissent, si eodem Pseudo-Isidoro auctore accessissent.
chunks.english: This is sufficiently refuted especially by manuscript Vatican six hundred thirty, in which, since the later additions of this same Pseudo-Isidore are found described in a double appendix at the end, we cannot doubt that those additional documents from the aforementioned manuscripts would have been appended there with the rest if they had been added by the same Pseudo-Isidore as author.
chunks.notes: Argues these forgeries were added by a different hand than Pseudo-Isidore

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc ergo Pseudo-Isidoro minus recte ascribuntur supposititiae epistolae tres, una Damasi ad Hieronymum, alia Hieronymi ad Damasum, et alia Leonis ad episcopos Germaniarum atque Galliarum,
chunks.english: Therefore the three forged letters—one from Damasus to Jerome, another from Jerome to Damasus, and another from Leo to the bishops of the Germanies and Gaul—are incorrectly attributed to Pseudo-Isidore,
chunks.notes: Correcting previous scholarly attribution

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: quae a puris Isidorianis codicibus absunt, et solum in iis inveniuntur qui pluribus nominibus a pura Isidoriana collectione distant et aliquot additamenta aliunde recepere.
chunks.english: since they are absent from the pure Isidorian manuscripts and are found only in those that diverge in many respects from the pure Isidorian collection and have received certain additions from elsewhere.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Non solus Pseudo-Isidorus fuit apocryphorum fabricator, sed alii plures ejusdem indolis inventi sunt.
chunks.english: Pseudo-Isidore was not the only fabricator of apocryphal texts; many others of the same character have been discovered.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ut autem omittamus quasdam vetustiores imposturas, epistola Deusdedit papae ad Gordianum Hispalensem episcopum cum aliis tribus quae in ea allegantur, Julii, Innocentii et Coelestini ad episcopos Isauriae, Ephesiorum, et Hierosolymae, suppositionem manifestam prodit; nec tamen in ullo Isidori codice inventa, huic artifici tribui potest.
chunks.english: But to pass over certain older impostures, the letter of Pope Deusdedit to Gordianus, Bishop of Seville, together with three others cited in it—from Julius, Innocent, and Celestine to the bishops of Isauria, Ephesus, and Jerusalem—reveals an obvious forgery. Yet since it is not found in any manuscript of Isidore, it cannot be attributed to this artificer.
chunks.notes: Deusdedit I was pope 615-618

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem est de quibusdam aliis commentitiis scriptis, de quibus in Additionibus Isidorianis dicetur capite sequenti.
chunks.english: The same is true of certain other fabricated writings, which will be discussed in the additions to Isidore in the following chapter.
chunks.notes: Foreshadows next section

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Codex Florentinus S. Marci signatus num. 182, qui saeculo circiter XII scriptus, et epistolas, et canones continet, in postrema parte a pura Isidoriana collectione aliquantulum discrepat.
chunks.english: The Florence manuscript of Saint Mark numbered one hundred eighty-two, written around the twelfth century and containing both letters and canons, diverges somewhat from the pure Isidorian collection in its final part.
chunks.notes: San Marco library in Florence

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam post Innocentii epistolas addit legem imperatoris Constantii ad Volusianum, et ipsius Volusiani edictum in causa Pelagianorum, quae in collectione canonum hoc tomo edenda leguntur cap. 19 et 20.
chunks.english: For after the letters of Innocent it adds the law of Emperor Constantius to Volusianus and Volusianus's own edict concerning the case of the Pelagians, which are read in the collection of canons to be published in this volume, chapters nineteen and twenty.
chunks.notes: Pelagian controversy (early 5th century)

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dein in Leone praeter ordinem ab Isidorianis codicibus diversum nonnullas epistolas praefert quae in iisdem codicibus desunt; et epistolam 12 ad Africanos Mauros non decurtatam ut illi, sed integram et exacte descriptam exhibet, qualem in nulla alia collectione invenire licuit.
chunks.english: Then in Leo, besides an order different from the Isidorian manuscripts, it presents several letters that are lacking in those manuscripts. And letter twelve to the African Moors it presents not in abridged form as they do, but complete and exactly copied, such as has not been possible to find in any other collection.
chunks.notes: Important witness to a fuller text

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vide praefationem in Leonis epistolas § 14, ubi quatuor et viginti epistolas, quae in eo codice describuntur, recensuimus.
chunks.english: See the preface to the letters of Leo, section fourteen, where we have enumerated the twenty-four letters that are copied in that manuscript.
chunks.notes: Cross-reference to earlier section of this work

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum autem epistola 24 ob codicem mutilum fine careat, num aliae Leonis epistolae sequerentur, et num in caeteris pontificibus aliquid aliud ab Isidori collectione diversum insereretur, ignoramus.
chunks.english: However, since letter twenty-four lacks its ending because the manuscript is damaged, we do not know whether other letters of Leo would follow, or whether anything else different from Isidore's collection would be inserted among the other pontiffs.
chunks.notes: Acknowledging limitations due to manuscript damage

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plura dicenda essent de ms. cod. Vat. 1340, et de duobus similibus Venetis S. Marci 168 et 169, qui in postrema item parte Isidorianae collectionis plurima documenta partim inserta, partim in fine addita continent. Sed cum haec latius pateant, de his separatim agendum erit capite sequenti.
chunks.english: More would need to be said about manuscript Vatican thirteen forty and about two similar ones in Venice at Saint Mark's, numbers one hundred sixty-eight and one hundred sixty-nine, which in the final part of the Isidorian collection likewise contain very many documents, partly inserted and partly added at the end. But since these matters are more extensive, they must be treated separately in the following chapter.
chunks.notes: Promises fuller discussion in next chapter

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Codex Vat. 629 saeculi XII, in-folio, ante Isidorianam collectionem quaedam praemittit S. Isidori Hispalensis opuscula, et Ecclesiasticam Rufini Historiam.
chunks.english: Vatican manuscript six hundred twenty-nine, from the twelfth century, in folio format, prefixes before the Isidorian collection certain short works of Saint Isidore of Seville and the Ecclesiastical History of Rufinus.
chunks.notes: Rufinus of Aquileia (c.345-411), translator and church historian

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sequitur Pseudo-Isidori praefatio. Dein index capitulorum cum hoc titulo: Incipit breviarium canonum apostolorum et primorum a S. Clemente usque ad S. Damasum apostolicorum sequens ordinem suum, quorum etiam gesta in hoc opere condita continentur subjecta capitulis suis.
chunks.english: There follows the preface of Pseudo-Isidore. Then an index of chapters with this title: 'Here begins the summary of the canons of the apostles and of the first apostolic men from Saint Clement up to Saint Damasus, following their proper order, whose deeds also preserved in this work are contained under their respective chapters.'
chunks.notes: Reflects structure of this manuscript's organization

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Inquit autem usque ad Damasum, quia post canones apostolorum epistolae omnes Isidorianae collectionis a S. Clemente ad Damasum usque continua serie describuntur, omissis intermediis canonibus conciliorum, nec non tractatu de primitiva Ecclesia et synodo Nicaena.
chunks.english: It says 'up to Damasus' because after the canons of the apostles, all the letters of the Isidorian collection from Saint Clement to Damasus are copied in continuous series, with the intermediate canons of councils omitted, as well as the treatise on the primitive Church and the Synod of Nicaea.
chunks.notes: Shows this manuscript omits conciliar material from first section

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In reliquis autem pontificibus librarius defatigatus multa omisit, pauca vero adjecit.
chunks.english: But among the remaining pontiffs, the weary scribe omitted much and added little.
chunks.notes: Common scribal fatigue in long manuscripts

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam unam tantum Siricii epistolam dedit ad Himerium, unam spuriam Anastasii ad Germaniae et Burgundiae episcopos, unam Innocentii ad Decentium, unam Zosimi ad Hesychium, Bonifacii epistolam ad Honorium Augustum et hujus rescriptum, unam Coelestini ad episcopos Galliae, quae incipit apostolici verba praecepti,
chunks.english: For he gave only one letter of Siricius to Himerius, one spurious letter of Anastasius to the bishops of Germany and Burgundy, one of Innocent to Decentius, one of Zosimus to Hesychius, the letter of Boniface to the Emperor Honorius and his response, one of Celestine to the bishops of Gaul beginning 'the words of the apostolic precept,'
chunks.notes: Lists selective copying of papal letters

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: unam apocrypham Xysti III ad Orientales, Leonis tres tantum ad Rusticum, ad Anastasium et ad Nicetam, unam Hilari ad Ascanium Tarraconensem, quae in Conciliis est ipsius pontificis epist. 2, omnes ex Isidoro excerptas.
chunks.english: one apocryphal letter of Sixtus the Third to the Easterners, only three of Leo—to Rusticus, to Anastasius, and to Nicetas—one of Hilary to Ascanius of Tarragona, which in the Councils is letter two of that pontiff, all excerpted from Isidore.
chunks.notes: Shows selective extraction from Isidorian corpus

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Duas addidit Simplicii, quae in Isidoro desunt, unam ad Joannem episcopum Ravennatem, alteram ad Florentium, Equitium et Severum episcopos, quae in Conciliis sunt ejusdem pontificis epist. 2 et 3, ac ex Hadriana collectione excerptae videntur.
chunks.english: He added two of Simplicius that are lacking in Isidore: one to John, Bishop of Ravenna, and another to Bishops Florentius, Equitius, and Severus, which in the Councils are letters two and three of that pontiff and appear to be excerpted from the Hadrianic collection.
chunks.notes: Shows mixture of Isidorian and Hadrianic sources

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Mox ex eadem collectione subduntur Felicis decreta in synodo, Flavio Boetio v. c. consule, etc., uti tom. V Concil., col. 275. Apud Isidorum sola epistola eidem synodo inserta et Siculis inscripta legitur.
chunks.english: Next from the same collection are added the decrees of Felix in synod under Flavius Boethius, honorable man and consul, and so forth, as in volume five of the Councils, column two hundred seventy-five. In Isidore only the letter inserted in that synod and addressed to the Sicilians is read.
chunks.notes: Felix III (II), pope 483-492

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postea vero ex Isidoro profertur alia tantum ejusdem Felicis epistola sexta ad Acacium, una Gelasii ad episcopos Lucaniae, primum Symmachi concilium, una tantum Hormisdae epist. 25 ad episcopos Hispanos,
chunks.english: But afterward from Isidore only one other letter of the same Felix is brought forward, the sixth to Acacius, one of Gelasius to the bishops of Lucania, the first council of Symmachus, only one letter of Hormisdas, number twenty-five, to the bishops of Spain,
chunks.notes: Selective extraction continues through early 6th century popes

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: una supposititia Joannis I ad Zachariam archiepiscopum, ac tandem una Felicis III al. IV ad episcopos diversarum provinciarum, quae apud Isidorum integra, hic prooemio diminuta legitur: incipit enim ab ultima ejusdem prooemii periodo: Scripta sanctitatis vestrae, quae ad sedem apostolicam, etc.
chunks.english: one spurious letter of John the First to Archbishop Zacharias, and finally one of Felix the Third, also called the Fourth, to bishops of various provinces, which in Isidore is complete but here is read with the preface shortened, for it begins with the last sentence of that preface: 'The writings of your holiness which to the apostolic see,' and so forth.
chunks.notes: Felix IV, pope 526-530 (numbering confusion due to Felix II antipope)

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praeterea singulis pontificibus ante cujusque epistolas suum elogium praemittitur ex Pontificali Romano, quod Anastasio bibliothecario tribuitur: et licet post laudatum Felicem nullae subsequentium pontificum constitutiones in eodem codice describantur; elogia tamen omnium afferuntur ad Hadrianum usque.
chunks.english: Moreover, before the letters of each pontiff his own eulogy is prefixed from the Roman Pontifical attributed to Anastasius the Librarian. And although after the aforementioned Felix no constitutions of subsequent pontiffs are copied in the same manuscript, nevertheless eulogies of all are provided up to Hadrian.
chunks.notes: Liber Pontificalis biographical notices added

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Succedunt capitula decem Gregorii VII, quorum initium: Quicunque militum, vel cujuscunque ordinis, vel professionis persona praedia ecclesiastica, etc., ut in concilio Romano celebrato an. 1078.
chunks.english: There follow ten chapters of Gregory the Seventh, beginning 'Whosoever of soldiers or of any order or profession holding ecclesiastical properties,' and so forth, as in the Roman council celebrated in the year ten seventy-eight.
chunks.notes: Gregory VII, reform pope (1073-1085)

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dein ordo de discretione poenitentium, quem Gregorius papa constituit. Ponunt canones peccantibus de quibusdam peccatis tres annos in poenitentia, unum ex his in pane et aqua, de quibusdam quinque annos, etc. Finis est, vitam consequi possimus sempiternam. Hunc ordinem hactenus nullibi invenimus.
chunks.english: Then the order concerning the discernment of penitents, which Pope Gregory established. The canons assign to sinners for certain sins three years of penance, one of these on bread and water, for certain sins five years, and so forth. The ending is 'that we may attain eternal life.' This order we have found nowhere else thus far.
chunks.notes: Unique penitential text; unclear which Gregory

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ultimum documentum est synodus Urbani II papae Placentiae habita an 1095, quae exstat tom. X Concil., col. 824.
chunks.english: The final document is the synod of Pope Urban the Second held at Piacenza in the year ten ninety-five, which is found in volume ten of the Councils, column eight hundred twenty-four.
chunks.notes: Urban II, pope 1088-1099; Council of Piacenza just before First Crusade

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Codex Vallicellanus D 38, saeculi circiter decimi, canonibus conciliorum omissis, Isidorianae collectionis epistolas continet a S. Clemente ad Damasum usque, ut in praecedenti codice.
chunks.english: Vallicellian manuscript D thirty-eight, from around the tenth century, with the canons of councils omitted, contains the letters of the Isidorian collection from Saint Clement to Damasus, as in the preceding manuscript.
chunks.notes: Vallicelliana library in Rome

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praeter Pseudo-Isidori praefationem habet etiam opusculum de primitiva Ecclesia. Subjicit Nicolai I epistolas in causa Rothadi episcopi Suessionensis, quae in editione Conciliorum leguntur.
chunks.english: Besides the preface of Pseudo-Isidore, it also has the short work on the primitive Church. It appends the letters of Nicholas the First in the case of Rothad, Bishop of Soissons, which are read in the edition of the Councils.
chunks.notes: Nicholas I (858-867); Rothad case was major 9th century controversy

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Codicem claudit epistola Vitaliani papae ad Paulum archiepiscopum Cretensem, quae in conciliis est ejusdem pontificis epist. I.
chunks.english: The manuscript concludes with the letter of Pope Vitalian to Paul, Archbishop of Crete, which in the councils is letter one of that pontiff.
chunks.notes: Vitalian, pope 657-672

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Duo codices Vat. 3788 saeculi XI, et 4873 aevi recentioris, easdem epistolas Isidorianas praeferunt a S. Clemente usque ad Damasum. Postea quaedam subnectunt ex S. Gregorio Magno quae in Isidoro non inveniuntur.
chunks.english: Two manuscripts—Vatican thirty-seven eighty-eight from the eleventh century and forty-eight seventy-three from a later period—present the same Isidorian letters from Saint Clement to Damasus. Afterward they append certain materials from Saint Gregory the Great that are not found in Isidore.
chunks.notes: Pattern of adding post-Isidorian Gregorian material

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Duo codices Isidoriani, qui Patavii in bibliotheca S. Joannis in Viridario continentur notati numeris 100 et 159, alia atque alia ab Isidoro aliena receperunt, quae non vacavit in adversaria referre:
chunks.english: Two Isidorian manuscripts held at Padua in the library of Saint John in Viridario, marked with numbers one hundred and one hundred fifty-nine, have received various materials foreign to Isidore, which we have not had leisure to record in our notes.
chunks.notes: San Giovanni in Verdara monastery, Padua

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: nec dubitamus quin alii codices alibi sint, aliquot omissionibus vel additamentis diversi. Ex diligenti autem quam § 5 dedimus purae Isidorianae collectionis descriptione, quid Isidorianum, quid addititium vel omissum sit, quisque facile intelliget.
chunks.english: Nor do we doubt that there are other manuscripts elsewhere, differing by various omissions or additions. But from the careful description we gave in section five of the pure Isidorian collection, anyone can easily understand what is Isidorian and what is an addition or omission.
chunks.notes: Concluding summary and cross-reference
